name: Aqueduct at Vicus Augustanus
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 B3 unnamed aqueduct (Vicus Augustanus)

name: Aqueduct at Vorgium
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 7 C3 unnamed aqueduct (Vorgium)

name: Barbegal
description: At Barbegal in southern France are the remains of a Roman watermill complex.

name: El Habel tunnel (Saldae aqueduct)
description: An aqueduct tunnel, near Saldae, sometimes referred to as the Nonius Datus tunnel. It was a component of a Roman aqueduct that delivered water from the area of modern Toudja to Saldae (modern Bejaia in Algeria's Kabylia province). The tunnel's modern name derives from the nearby village of El Habel, which is located just north of Meccuc in the municipal district of Bejaia.

name: Jerwan Aqueduct
description: The Assyrian king Sennacherib had an aqueduct constructed between 703 and 690 B.C. near the ancient city of Girmua. The structure is part of the "Khinnis [Canal] System." The identification of the structure's builder is confirmed by means of several inscriptions.

name: Perrotte Aqueduct tunnel, aqueduct of Nimes
description: The aqueduct tunnel known as the Tunnel de la Perrotte stretches for more than 60 meters.

name: Segovia aqueduct
description: Supplied Segovia with water from mountains to the southeast

name: Unnamed aqueduct at Angitiae Lucus
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 D2 unnamed aqueduct (Angitiae Lucus)

name: Unnamed Aqueduct at Cillium
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 33 C2 unnamed aqueduct (at Cillium)

name: Unnamed aqueduct at Ephesus (Aqua Iulia?)
description: The longest and most recently-constructed Roman aqueduct in the province of Asia, measuring 36.5 km from its source in the hills south of Ephesus to the metropolis itself. The aqueduct was first commissioned by the emperor Hadrian in 122-126, and it portions of it remained in use into the medieval period. In modern times, it is known as the Değirmendere Aqueduct, after the nearby Kenchrios river (modern Değirmendere).

name: Unnamed aqueduct supplying Rusicade/Thapsus
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 31 F3 unnamed aqueduct (Rusicade and spring)

name: Aqueduct at Tarracinae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 D3 unnamed aqueduct (Tarracina(e))

name: Aqueduct near Šinuhtu
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 66 F1 unnamed aqueduct (Tyana)

name: Aqueduct at Sybaris
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 46 D2 unnamed aqueduct (Thurii/Copia)

name: Aqueduct W of Zliten
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 G2 unnamed aqueduct (Lepcis)

name: Aqueduct at Kepos
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 52 D2 unnamed aqueduct (“Aqueduct of Theodosius”, NNW Constantinopolis)

name: Aqueduct at Pineda de Mar
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 H4 unnamed aqueduct (Pineda de Mar, SW Blanda(e))

name: Tigris Tunnel Inscriptions
description: The so-called Tigris Tunnel is a cave ca. fifty miles north of Diyarbakır, Turkey and it forms a source of the Tigris River. In its vicinity there are several Assyrian monuments, including five inscriptions.

name: Actium Victory Monument
description: A naval victory monument was built by Octavian at Nicopolis (Epirus) to celebrate the Battle of Actium.

name: Adamkayalar
description: A site of rock-cut relief sculpture, perhaps dating to the second century.

name: Agdistis Sanctuary (Yazılıkaya)
description: A plateau around a monumental Phrygian rock‐cut altar at the extensive fortified settlement and sanctuary complex of Yazılıkaya in Turkey. Associated with Agdistis in the 20th century because of Roman epigraphic finds there, more recent excavations have demonstrated that the sanctuary was in use from the 8th–7th century BCE to the Roman Period (1st-2nd century CE). Continuity of ritual practice in front of the rock-cut altar is demonstrated by rock-cut bows, a ritual preparation area with hearths, a rock-cut statue identifiable as the goddess Matar, and the Roman-era votive inscriptions.

name: Agora of Onchestos
description: Approximate center point of agora of Onchestos sanctuary visible in satellite imagery.

name: Aianteion
description: Aianteion refers to a place on a promontory near İn Tepe where classical sources believed Telamonian Ajax was buried.

name: Aigaionos Monu.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 52 C4 Aigaionos Monu.

name: Al-Khazneh
description: An elaborate, rock-cut monument at Petra built ca. 100 BC to AD 200 known as "Al-Khazneh" or "The Treasury". The Nabatean structure, originally used as a tomb, dates to the reign of Aretas IV Philopatris.

name: Ale's Stones
description: A megalithic monument in Sweden in the form of a ship, likely dating from ca. AD 600, the end of the Nordic Iron Age.

name: Allianoi
description: An ancient spa, known in modern times as Paşa Ilıca in Turkey.

name: Altar of Androgeos
description: The Altar of Androgeos, son of Minos, at Phaleron is described by Pausanias.

name: Apa Phoibammon (monastery)
description: The seventh-century monastery of St. Phoibammon was built on the upper terrace of the temple of Hatshepsut at Deir el-Bahri-

name: Apotheke
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 C3 Apotheke

name: Aquae Flavianae
description: Aquae Flavianae, a place in Numidia (modern Fontaine-Chaude near Khenchela).

name: Aquae Sinuessanae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 E3 Aquae Sinuessanae

name: Arae incendii Neroniani
description: A series of altars commemorated the "Great Fire of 64" that occurred during the reign of the emperor Nero. These so-called "Arae incendii Neroniani" are assigned to the reign of Domitian. The original number of altars is unknown and debated. Epigraphy aids greatly in a reconstruction of this distributed monument, as does the in situ altar on the Quirinal Hill. The latter was originally situated opposite the temple of Quirinus and now lies beneath the Ministero della Casa Reale, near the modern church of S. Andrea.

name: Arch at F Street and 10th Street
description: Monumentalized passage arch marking the intersection of F Street and the south side of 10th Street in Dura-Europos.

name: Arch of Alexander Severus
description: The Arch of Alexander Severus at Thugga was built in 228.

name: Arch of Augustus (Fano)
description: A city gate in the form of a triumphal arch with three vaults. The gate marks the entry of the via Flaminia into the city.

name: Arch of Augustus (Pompeii)
description: The brick core of an arch monument stands immediately to the west of the Temple of Jupiter in the Forum of Pompeii. It has had various appellations including "Arch of Drusus", referring to Drusus Iulius Caesar the son of emperor Tiberius.

name: Arch of Augustus (Susa)
description: An arch dedicated to Augustus at Susa in the late first century BC that records the renewed alliance between the princeps and Marcus Julius Cottius.

name: Arch of Augustus at Aosta
description: The Arch of Augustus at Aosta is a commemorative arch dedicated to Augustus ca. 25 BC following victories over the Salassi.

name: Arch of Augustus at Ariminum
description: The Arch of Augustus at Ariminum was dedicated in 27 B.C. and marked the end of the Via Flaminia.

name: Arch of Caligula (Pompeii)
description: The so-called Arch of Caligula has been referred to by various names and is unlikely to be connected with the emperor Caius. It is located near the intersection of the Via di Mercurio with Via delle Terme and Via della Fortuna.

name: Arch of Caracalla
description: The Arch of Caracalla at Volubilis was constructed in 217 CE by the governor Marcus Aurelius Sebastenus in honor of the emperor Caracalla and his mother Domna Julia.

name: Arch of Caracalla
description: The so-called Arch of Caracalla at Theveste is a tetrapylon arch dating to the third century. Originally dedicated to the deified Septimius Severus with Julia Domna and Caracalla, the arch was repurposed and modified to function as a gate in the Byzantine city wall.

name: Arch of Caracalla, Djemila
description: A Roman triumphal arch monument dedicated to Caracalla, Julia Domna, and the deified Septimius Severus located at ancient Cuicul / Curculum (modern Djémila).

name: Arch of Constantine
description: The Arch of Constantine at Rome, a triumphal arch dedicated in A.D. 315.

name: Arch of Diocletian
description: The Arch of Diocletian dates to the late third century.

name: Arch of Galerius
description: The extant remains of a triumphal arch, constructed from 298 to 299 CE and dedicated in 303 CE under the tetrarch Galerius. It commemorates the capture of Ctesiphon from the Sassanids and forms part of a monumental entrance to the palace of Galerius.

name: Arch of Gallienus
description: The Arch of Gallienus marked the Porta Esquilina, a gate in the Servian Wall of Rome. The via Labicana and via Tiburtina issue from Rome at this point. The arch was erected in A.D. 262.

name: Arch of Germanicus
description: A triumphal arch built by C. Julius Rufus, and dedicated to Tiberius and his adoptive sons, Drusus Caesar and Germanicus.

name: Arch of Hadrian at Athens
description: The Arch of Hadrian is a monument in the form of a Roman triumphal arch, constructed by the citizens of Athens in AD 131 or 132 to honor the Roman emperor Hadrian.

name: Arch of Lucius Neratius Priscus
description: An arch dedicated to Lucius Neratius Priscus, suffect consul of A.D. 97 and governor of Germania Inferior.

name: Arch of Malborghetto
description: A fourth-century A.D. tetrapylon arch stands at the intersection of the Via Flaminia and a road linking the Via Tiberina and Veii.

name: Arch of Marcus Antonius
description: The so-called "Arch of Marcus Antonius" takes its name from a local tradition that the citizens of Aquinum built the monument in a single day to honor Antonius. In all likelihood, it is a monument connected either to a deductio of veterans sent to Aquinum in 41 BCE or to the work of Caius Calvisius Sabinus who reorganized the via Latina in 27 BCE.

name: Arch of Marcus Aurelius at Tripoli
description: A quadrifons arch at ancient Oea (modern Tripoli) that commemorates Roman victories over the Parthians in A.D. 161.

name: Arch of Septimius Severus
description: The Arch of Septimius Severus is situated at the northwest corner of the Forum Romanum. It was dedicated in A.D. 203 and serves to commemorate the Parthian victories of Emperor Septimius Severus and his two sons against the Parthians (194/195 and 197–199).

name: Arch of Septimius Severus
description: The Arch of Septimius Severus dates to 205.

name: Arch of Septimius Severus at Lepcis Magna
description: The Arch of Septimius Severus at Lepcis Magna dates ca. A.D. 203.

name: Arch of the Sergii
description: A Roman triumphal arch at Pula commemorating Lucius Sergius Lepidus and the family of the Sergii for service at the Battle of Actium in 31 BC.

name: Arch of Titus
description: A triumphal arch erected in summa Sacra Via and dedicated to the deified emperor Titus by his brother Domitian, ca. 81 CE.

name: Arch of Trajan
description: An arch dedicated to Trajan on the Via Flaminia at Carsulae, also known as the Arco di San Damiano.

name: Arch of Trajan
description: The Arch of Trajan at Maktar.

name: Arch of Trajan at Ancona
description: An arch erected to honor Trajan between A.D. 100 and 116.

name: Arch of Trajan at Benevento
description: A triumphal arch built in honor of Trajan at Beneventum; it marks the Trajanic extension of the Via Appia.

name: Arch of Trajan at Canosa
description: The Arch of Trajan at Canosa dates to the second century CE.

name: Arco Augusteo di Siracusa
description: The remains of a Roman arch at Syracuse dated soon after the deduction of the Roman colony of 21 BCE.

name: Arco de los Blanco
description: A Roman gateway.

name: Arco dei Gavi
description: A Roman arch built by the gens Gavia.

name: Arco del Sacramento
description: A Roman arch of the second century A.D. that faced on to the forum of Beneventum.

name: Arco di San Lazzaro
description: The so-called Arch of Saint Lazarus is part of a ruined arcuated structure located in Rome along Via Marmorata. Since this is the general vicinity of the ancient Emporium, these remains may belong to an ancient horreum. Medieval folklore associated the building with Horatius Cocles.

name: Arco di Trevi
description: An opus quadratum monument with an arched gateway perhaps marking a boundary between the territory of the Equi and the Hernicans.

name: Arcus Argentariorum
description: A commemorative arch located in the Velabrum dedicated to the Severan emperors in AD 204 by the local association of money-changers (argentarii) and merchants (negotiantes). It was later incorporated into the church of S. Giorgio al Velabro.

name: Arcus Dolabellae et Silani
description: Situated on the Caelian Hill, the Arcus Dolabellae et Silani sits at the northern corner of the Castra Peregrina. The gate dates to A.D. 10 and is likely a rebuilding of an earlier gate that was part of the so-called Servian Wall, perhaps the Porta Querquetulana (or Querquetularia) or the Porta Caelimontana.

name: Arcus Novus
description: A now-lost arch on the ancient Via Lata. It was dedicated to Diocletian either in 293 CE (marking the occasion of his decennalia) or in 303-304 (joint triumph with Maximian). The present church of Santa Maria in Via Lata occupies the site of the arch.

name: Arcus Tiberii
description: A single-bay triumphal arch dedicated to Tiberius in 16 CE. This arch commemorated the recovery of Varus' standards lost in 9 CE. It stood on the Sacra Via at the northwest corner of the Basilica Iulia.

name: Area di Monte Giulio e Capitaneria
description: The low hill known as Monte Giulio is anthropic, formed by the accumulation of sediment atop an ancient quay. Archaeological investigation beginning in 1968 revealed numerous architectural remains, including a cistern and a bath complex along the via Guidoni. The ruins of the so-called Capitaneria, or Harbor office, are located near the modern stele to the Fallen of Kindu. This complex seems to have been connected to the management of port traffic and was in use from the reign of Claudius until the fourth century.

name: Arpaia milestone
description: A Roman milestone of the Via Appia located in Arpaia, Italy.

name: Arras-sur-Rhône Roman Milestone
description: A Roman milestone located in the modern French commune of Arras-sur-Rhône, registered as a historical monument in 1926 when it was to be found in the cemetery of the church of Saint Clair.

name: Arslan Kaya monument
description: The rock-cut "Arslan Kaya monument" located just west of Lake Emre in Turkey is in the Phrygian Highlands. The monument carries an inscription in Old Phrygian to the goddess Materan.

name: Artemis Thermia, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 C3 Artemis Thermia, T.

name: Arx of Cosa
description: The citadel of the ancient city and location of its Capitolium.

name: Asklepieion at Zea
description: A sanctuary of Asklepios above Zea harbour in Piraeus, founded in the late 5th century BC and active through the Imperial period. Sections of the sanctuary were explored archaeologically in the 1880s and 2000s.

name: Assyrian rock relief at Yağmur
description: Inscribed relief, dated to the reign of Tiglath-Pileser I, on a large block near the fortified site of Yağmur in the Tur 'Abdin.

name: Augusteum, Antioch in Pisidia
description: The Augusteum, or Sebasteion, at Antioch in Pisidia was a monumental architectural complex dedicated to the Roman Imperial cult. Its archaeological remains were first excavated in 1913. Approximately 60 fragments of the Res Gestae Divi Augusti were discovered at the site.

name: Avebury stone circle
description: Avebury is a Neolithic henge monument comprised of three stone circles dating to the third millennium BCE.

name: Bagistanus (mountain)
description: Mount Behistun is located in the Kermanshah Province of Iran. It is the site of the so-called Behistun Inscription, a multilingual inscription and rock relief established by Darius the Great (r. 522–486 B.C.).

name: Barm-i Dilak
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C4 Barm-i Dilak

name: Barreira Megalithic Complex
description: A prehistoric megalithic complex in the modern Sintra municipality in Portugal's Lisbon district. It was formally identified in 1961 and designated as a cultural heritage site in 1993. The complex consists of at least 24 standing stones.

name: Batas-Harir
description: A first-century CE rock-cut relief at Batas-Harir is likely associated with the Parthian culture and depicts a ruler, perhaps the king Abdissares or Izates II or III of Adiabene. The site is located in Iraqi Kurdistan in the Erbil Governorate.

name: Baths of Faverges
description: The Roman Baths of Faverges date to the first century.

name: Bavian/Khinnis
description: Just a few kilometers upstream of the city, the 7th-century B.C. Assyrian king Sennacherib had an architectural complex built and several stele-like panels (with accompanying inscriptions) carved into a nearby cliff face. The site may have served as a summer residence for the Assyrian king and may have been used as a headquarters to supervise the construction of a vast network of canals and aqueducts that was created specifically for providing water to Nineveh and the Assyrian heartland. The "Khinnis Reliefs" were included on the 2014 World Monuments Watch to increase awareness of cultural heritage in the Khinnis/Bavian area.

name: Beltany
description: At Beltany in County Donegal, Ireland, is a Neolithic stone circle monument comprised of 64 menhirs. The monument dates ca. 1400-800 BC.

name: Berà
description: A Roman triumphal arch dedicated ca. 13 BC by Lucius Licinius Sura and perhaps dedicated to Augustus.

name: Bolnisi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 88 C3 Bolnisi

name: Bouleuterion of Onchestos
description: The Bouleuterion of Onchestos has been identified adjacent to the Temple of Poseidon.

name: Braq
description: Khirbet Braq is the modern name of a ruined Nabatean sanctuary at Ayn Braq in Jordan, a spring that, in antiquity, was channeled through an aqueduct to supply ancient Petra. The heavily looted site is located just south of modern Wadi Musa, on the west side of the modern road to Taybeh.

name: Bronze Athena/Athena Promachos
description: A colossal bronze statue of Athena on the Acropolis of Athens, built in the mid-5th century BC by Pheidias. The base was repaired in the time of Augustus. In Late Antiquity, the statue was transferred to Constantinople, where it was destroyed in 1203.

name: Buddhas of Bamyan
description: Monumental statues of Gautam Buddha were carved into the cliff-face at Bamyan, located in the Bamyan valley in the Hazarajat region of central Afghanistan, The statues were, respectively, 35 and 53 m tall. They were intentionally destroyed by the Taliban in 2001.

name: Bulls of Guisando
description: A series of Celtiberian sculptures located near El Tiemblo, Ávila, Spain. These statues were perhaps originally created during the second century BC.

name: Cabanes
description: The remains of a Roman arch consisting of two columns supported by a square base.

name: Caepionis Monu.
description: Caepionis Monumentum (Tower of Caepio) was a tower-cum-lighthouse built by the Roman proconsul Quintus Servilius Caepio in 140 BC. The aim was to make the Salmedina reef at the mouth of the river Baitis (Guadalquivir) navigable. The modern toponym is Chipiona.

name: Casteddu di Ceccia
description: A stone-built monument of the Torrean civilization located south of Porto-Vecchio, Corsica.

name: Casteddu di Tappa
description: Casteddu di Tappa is a Bronze Age archaeological site in Corsica connected with the Torréen culture.

name: Castell Bryn Gwyn
description: Castell Bryn Gwyn is a circular Neolithic enclosure some 54 m in diameter that shows signs of re-use during the Iron Age and in the Roman period. The site is located on the Isle of Anglesey.

name: Cat Stane
description: An irregular standing stone bearing a fragmentary Latin inscription, the Cat Stane is likely a Neolithic monument re-used for a burial of the fifth or sixth century AD.

name: Caveirac milestone
description: A Roman milestone - (CIL 17-02, no. 256).

name: Chanctonbury Ring
description: Chanctonbury Ring is a prehistoric hill fort located on Chanctonbury Hill in West Sussex, England. It is part of the present-day South Downs Way.

name: Chapel of Wadjmose
description: Chapel dedicated to the prince Wadjmose, son of Pharaoh Thutmose I.

name: Chil Zena
description: A mountainous outcropping located to the west of Kandahar, Afghanistan. A flight of forty stone steps leads to the top of the outcropping. The bilingual inscription of the third century BCE known as the Kandahar Bilingual Rock Inscription is found here.

name: Choragic Monument of Lysicrates
description: The Choragic Monument of Lysicrates is a round structure erected in 335/4 BC in Athens to honor the victory of a choregos in a dramatic competition.

name: Clickimin broch
description: A Broch tower with other associated Iron Age structures.

name: Collegium at Italica
description: A building that has been identified as the seat of a Collegium because of its internal structure and size, was previously known as the"House of the Exedra".

name: Colossus Neronis
description: The "Colossus Neronis" or "Colossal Statue of Nero" was a monumental bronze statue of the first-century CE emperor reportedly created by Zenodorus. The statue was originally an element of the sprawling Domus Aurea of Nero but the monument endured long after Nero's reign. The statue's original position likely marked the summit of the Velian Hill. The Flavians refashioned the statue to resemble Sol and Hadrian relocated the statue nearer the Flavian amphitheater as part of the work to create the Temple of Venus and Roma.

name: Column of Julian
description: The Column of Julianus, ca. 362 CE, honors the military exploits of the Roman Emperor Julian.

name: Column of Marcus Aurelius
description: The Column of Marcus Aurelius is a monumental victory column erected along the Via Lata between 176 and 193 CE. The monument celebrated victories realized against the Marcomanni and the Sarmatians (172-175 CE).

name: Column of Trajan
description: The Column of Trajan (or Trajan's Column) is a victory column that was erected in the Forum of Trajan at Rome ca. 113 in order to commemorate Trajan's victories in the Dacian Wars. The column is particularly noted for its helical historiated bas relief frieze decoration.

name: Demeter Temple (Aegae - Yuntdağı)
description: A temple complex at Aegae-Yuntdağı, associated with the cult of Demeter and Kore based on epigraphic evidence, may also have been sacred to other divinities. The site is located to the west of the Scaenae frons of the theater complex.

name: Djebel Taya
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 31 G3 Djebel Taya

name: Dolmen of Guadalperal
description: A megalithic stone circle dating to the third millennium BCE located in Peraleda de la Mata in eastern Extremadura, Spain. The monument is usually submerged beneath the waters of the Embalse de Valdecañas, a reservoir of the Tagus river.

name: Domus Aurea
description: A sprawling palace complex begun by Nero after A.D. 64 that aimed to connect the Esquiline, Caelian, and Palatine hills.

name: Dorset Cursus
description: A Neolithic cursus monument spanning some 10 km of Cranborne Chase in east Dorset, United Kingdom.

name: Drombeg stone circle
description: A megalithic stone circle monument, also known as the Druid's Altar, consisting of seventeen closely spaced stones with a diameter of 9.3 m. Thirteen stones survive today.

name: Elephas herbarius
description: Elephas herbarius (Elefantum herbarium), a place included in the Notitia, is mentioned only once and must lie at the southern limit of Regio VIII. Coarelli concludes that this place must have been located on the lower slopes of the Capitoline Hill facing the Forum Holitorium. Its coincidence with Late Antique and Medieval toponyms suggests that it refers to an ancient statue of an elephant, perhaps one shown eating plants, in the area of the Forum Holitorium.

name: Erechtheion
description: The Erechtheion is a temple on the north side of the Athenian Acropolis dedicated to Athena Polias and other deities and heroes; the temple is of the Ionic order with several distinctive features, including its famous caryatid porch.

name: Etruscan Pyramid of Bomarzo
description: The so-called Etruscan Pyramid of Bomarzo is a megalithic, rock-cut monument at Bomarzo. It likely dates to the late Archaic period.

name: Euthymios
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 70 G2 Euthymios

name: Fifth century baths at Lebadeia
description: Foundations of a bath facility with mosaic pavement and other structures were discovered during rescue excavations in 2006 at the corner of Daidalou and Kadmou streets in modern Levadeia, Greece.

name: Filitosa
description: Filitosa is a megalithic site in southern Corsica that was in use from the Neolithic period until Roman times. A number of menhirs have been discovered at the site.

name: Fines Africae et Cyrenensium
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 37 D2 Fines Africae et Cyrenensium

name: Forum antique de Bavay
description: The Gallo-Roman forum of ancient Bagacum (Bavay) covers more than 2.5 ha.

name: Forum Holitorium
description: The Forum Holitorium was the vegetable market of ancient Rome, situated outside the Porta Carmentalis in the southern Campus Martius. This area was bounded by the Forum Boarium and the Circus Flaminius.

name: Forum Romanum
description: The ancient Roman forum.

name: Gali Zardak
description: A group of badly preserved rock-cut sculptures located along a watercourse of the same name that drains the northern face of Jebel Maqlub.

name: Ganj Nameh
description: Ganj Nameh (or Gandj Nameh) is a site located approximately 12 km southwest of Ecbatana. Two trilingual Achaemenid royal inscriptions are to be found here. One of these was ordered by Darius the Great (r.  522–486 BCE) and the other by his son, Xerxes the Great (r.  486–465 BCE).

name: Garni temple
description: The Garni Temple dates to the first century A.D. and was sacred to the Armenian sun god Mihr.

name: Gate of Athena Archegetis
description: The Gate of Athena Archegetis is the Doric-order gateway at the west of the Roman Agora in Athens. It was dedicated to Athena Archigetis in 11/10 BCE.

name: Gavurkale
description: Gavurkale is a hill in central Anatolia with remains of both Hittite rock-cut reliefs and architecture dating from various periods.

name: Geziret el Marakeb
description: A long and confused tradition of the archaeology of Geziret el Marakeb or "Seal Island" is reviewed by Carter who debunks many of the earlier claims. The island is the site of curious stone enclosures as well as evidence for Late Roman activity.

name: Gianola
description: The site of a villa associated with Lucius Vitruvius Mamurra.

name: Gobustan Rock Art Cultural Landscape
description: The Gobustan Rock Art Cultural Landscape is a UNESCO World Heritage Site located in central Azerbaijan. The 537 ha site comprises more than 6,000 rock engravings covering a period of 40,000 years.

name: Gors Fawr stone circle
description: Gors Fawr Stone Circle is located in Mynachlog-Ddu, Pembrokeshire, Wales, United Kingdom.

name: Grand Lagneux Roman Bath
description: At Grand Lagneux, a small village in the French commune of Yenne, a rescue excavation in 2020 revealed part of a Roman-era bathing establishment. The excavated site is now buried under a modern home. This find constitutes another possible locality in the commune for attested ancient name "Etanna".

name: Grange stone circle
description: Grange stone circle or Lios na Grainsi is the largest stone circle in Ireland, consisting of 113 stones and was built ca. 2100 BC.

name: Greolieres Roman Milestone
description: A Roman milestone located in France, in the town of Gréolières in the Alpes-Maritimes department in the Provence-Alpes-Côte d'Azur region. The inscription on the milestone honors the Roman emperor Caracalla and is dated to 213.

name: Grotte dell'Acqua
description: Architectural remains near Lago Fusaro that may have been associated with a so-called "villa maritima".

name: Grutenhäuschen
description: The Grutenhäuschen is a Roman funereal temple of the third or fourth century A.D. It has been reconstructed on site.

name: Güglingen Mithraeum
description: A sanctuary dedicated to Mithras located near modern Heilbronn, Germany, in use during the second-third centuries CE.

name: Guyum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C4 Guyum

name: Heidentor
description: A triumphal monument built at Carnuntum between A.D. 354 and 361, perhaps by Constantius II.

name: Hellenistic Funerary Monument (Keryneia)
description: Also frequently referred to as "the Heroon" in archaeological literature, this structure at the site of Keryneia in Achaea was excavated in the mid-20th century.

name: Heraion
description: The sanctuary of Hera on Samos.

name: Hermes and Aphrodite Sanctuary
description: This large, open-air sanctuary near Syme Viannou in southern Crete was in use from 2000 BCE until at least the sixth century CE. It attracted pilgrims who are assumed to have camped at the site, which is far from any contemporary settlement. Monumental construction began in the early second millennium BCE and multiple phases and changes are evident, including the construction of Christian chapels in late antiquity. Although the identities of the deities worshiped here at the earliest periods are uncertain (as are the saints venerated in the Christian period), the association with Hermes and Aphrodite seems to have begun as early as the 10th century BCE.

name: Hipposthenis Monu.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 53 A2 Hipposthenis Monu.

name: Honorific arch
description: An honorific arch built in Pompeii near the Temple of Jupiter.

name: Horologium Augusti
description: The Horologium Augusti or Solarium Augusti was an ancient monument of the Campus Martius built during the reign of Augustus. In theory, it functioned as a giant solar marker. An obelisk relocated to Rome from Heliopolis served as the gnomon. The obelisk today stands in the Piazza di Monte Citorio.

name: Hung-i Kamalvand
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 93 F2 Hung-i Kamalvand

name: Hung-i Nauruzi/Hung-i Azdar
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 93 F2 Hung-i Nauruzi/Hung-i Azdar

name: Hung-i Yar-i 'Alivand
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 93 F2 Hung-i Yar-i 'Alivand

name: Hypereia (spring)
description: Strabo locates a Hypereia krēnē, mentioned in the Iliad's "Catalog of Ships", at Pherai in Thessaly (modern Velestino) and geographically criticizes its assignment by Homer to the contingent led by Eurypylos. Without comment, Pliny places "fons Hyperia" immediately before "oppida Pherae" in a list of places in Thessaly (E. Kirsten in RE accepts this as a reference to the spring at Pherai, but BAtlDir lists it as an "unlocated spring in Thessaly"). Archaeological work confirms a perennial spring at Velestino was a locus of settlement activity from the Neolithic period onwards, and a structure monumentalizing the spring was built and modified during the Classical and Hellenistic periods.

name: Ianus Augustus
description: The remains of a Roman arch may mark the boundary between the Roman provinces of Hispania Baetica and Hispania Tarraconensis.

name: Ianus Quadrifons
description: A quadrifrons triumphal arch at Rome that is located at a crossroads in the area of the Velabrum and the Forum Boarium. It dates to the early fourth century AD; its construction employed spolia, and it likely honored either Constantine I or Constantius II.

name: Imperatoris Hadriani Augusti Definitio Silvarum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 A5 Imperatoris Hadriani Augusti Definitio Silvarum

name: Investiture relief of Ardashir I
description: A rock-cut relief depicting the investiture of Ardashir I, ca. A.D. 226-242. This relief is the earliest Sasanian monument at Naqš-e Rustam.

name: Is Calas
description: A nuragic complex located near Monte Nai, Sardinia.

name: Is Calas menhir
description: A menhir located near the Is Calas nuragic complex near Monte Nai, Sardinia.

name: Jebel Oust
description: At Jebel Oust [Jbel al Wost] (Tunisia) are the remains of a large Roman bathing complex with a number of built cisterns.

name: Ka'ba-ye Zartosht
description: A stone monument located in the Naqsh-e Rustam compound.

name: Kalliroe
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 71 B2 Kalliroe

name: Kamouh el Hermel
description: An ancient pyramidal monument located 6 km south of Hermel in the Baalbek-Hermel Governorate, Lebanon. The monument likely dates to the second century BCE.

name: Karabel Reliefs
description: A monument consisting of multiple Hittite rock reliefs with Luwian inscriptions, probably dating to the 13th century BCE, located in the Karabel pass on the southern slopes of Mount Nif in Turkey's Izmir province. Most of the reliefs and inscriptions known to western scholars from the 19th and 20th centuries have been lost to road construction and vandalism.

name: Karaftu
description: At Karaftu is a series of rock-cut chambers with a Greek inscription, dating to the fourth or third century BCE. The site is located in Kordestān Province, Iran.

name: Karnak
description: A large temple complex in Egypt, associated with the ancient site of Thebes and Luxor and inscribed together with them on the UNESCO World Heritage list.

name: Kekropeion
description: The tomb and shrine of Kekrops, first king of Athens, located on the Acropolis. In the sixth century BC it was marked by a giant Ionic column, which was destroyed in the Persian Sack of 480 BC. The site was partially covered by the southwestern corner of the Erechtheion in the second half of the fifth century BC.

name: Kenk Boğazı
description: The site of an Assyrian relief of Shalmaneser III, which is located 10 km away from the Karasu relief.

name: Keskese
description: The archaeological site of Keskese is located approximately 8 km north of Matara. It is the site of numerous stelai dating ca. 500 BCE.

name: Khirbet ed Deir
description: The early Byzantine monastery located at Khirbet ed-Deir in the Judean desert was excavated from 1981-1987 by Yizhar Hirschfeld. The remote site yielded important evidence about monastic life from the fourth to seventh centuries CE.

name: Khufu's ship
description: The sealed burial pit adjacent to the Great Pyramid contained a remarkably well preserved Old Kingdom ship that has been interpreted as the solar barge of the pharaoh Khufu.

name: Khurha
description: A partially-excavated complex near modern Qum in Iran. The site was possibly a sanctuary and likely dates to the Seleucid age. There is some evidence that the site continued in use into the Parthian period.

name: Khwaja Ali Sehyaka/Sehyak
description: This "Mesopotamian-style temple" site located near modern Dishu in Afghanistan's Helmand province was excavated in the 1970s. Carbon-14 dates and "Greek-style architectural fragments and fine red pottery" led the excavators to date the use of the site from the late Hellenistic and Parthian period to "the beginning of the Sasanian period in the 3rd century" CE. Finds included a fragmentary bilingual (Greek/Aramaic) inscription possibly mentioning a Parthian king Phraates and the Iranian deity Ahuramazda.

name: Knocknakilla megaliths
description: A megalithic complex located between Macroom and Millstreet, in County Cork, Ireland, and thought to have been constructed ca. 3,500 years ago.

name: Kodrigai
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 C3 Kodrigai

name: Koh Ker
description: An ancient temple complex of the Khmer Empire, Koh Ker is also known as Lingapura or Chok Gargyar. The site is located in the Srayong commune of Preah, Vihear province's Kulen district. The site was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage site in September 2023 and covers some 1,187 ha.

name: Kom el-Dikka
description: Part of the ancient city of Alexandria where the Roman theater and baths are located.

name: La Montjoie
description: A monumental Gallo-Roman column.

name: Lacus Curtius
description: The Lacus Curtius is an area in medio foro connected with the earliest phases of Rome's occupation. By the late Republican period, it had become a small basin set inside a paved area. Livy relates the famous episode of the Sabine Mettius Curtius in relation to his description of the site.

name: Lanuéjols
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 15 A1 Lanuéjols

name: Lapis Niger
description: A shrine in the Forum Romanum adjacent to the Republican comitium, recognizable by the distinctive black paving stones that covered it in the later republican period. The shrine has been associated, in tradition, with the tomb of Romulus and/or of Faustulus. Giacomo Boni excavated the site between 1899 and 1905.

name: Lateran Obelisk
description: The Lateran Obelisk is a monumental Egyptian obelisk, completed during the reign of Thutmose IV (ca. 1400 BCE) and looted from Karnak in the early fourth century CE.

name: Les Vaux de la Celle
description: An ancient Gallo-Roman sanctuary near modern Genainville (France), with excavated remains of a temple, nymphaeum, and theater: BAtlas 11 B3 Génainville (sic)

name: Leuctra tropaion
description: A trophy monument erected by the Thebans following their defeat of the Spartans in 371 BC.

name: Lion of Amphipolis (monument)
description: The Lion of Amphipolis is a fourth-century BC tomb sculpture from Amphipolis in northern Greece. It may originally have been connected with the Kasta Tomb.

name: Maen Llia
description: Maen Llia is a standing stone located in the Brecon Beacons, Wales.

name: Maiden Tower (Baku)
description: A twelfth-century monument in the Old City, Baku, Azerbaijan.

name: Marmara sanctuary
description: An ancient sanctuary, perhaps sacred to Demeter, that was discovered during tunneling for the Corinth to Patras highway. The terraced site is located at Marmara, SSE of Aigeira. It dates from the seventh century BCE to the third century BCE.

name: Marmoutier Abbey
description: The Marmoutier Abbey located near Tours, Indre-et-Loire, France is also known as the Abbey of Marmoutier or Marmoutiers. Saint Martin of Tours (316-397) founded the abbey in 372 after he became Bishop of Tours in 371.

name: Matzanni
description: A sanctuary that includes a Punic temple built in white limestone, a nuraghe, and a sacred well.

name: Mausoleum of the First Qin Emperor
description: The mausoleum of Qin Shi Huang, the first emperor of the Qin dynasty of China, was built between 246 and 208 BCE. The mausoleum itself was outfitted with numerous ancillary features, including the famed "terracotta army" discovered in 1974. The site was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1987.

name: Meher Kapısı
description: Urartian inscribed rock niche.

name: Memnon Colossi
description: Two seated, colossal statues of the Pharaoh Amenhotep III at a site on the west bank of the Nile river.

name: Meteora
description: Metéora comprises a complex of six Eastern Orthodox monasteries built on natural sandstone pillars of the Thessalian plain. The site was listed as a UNESCO World Heritage site in 1988.

name: Meter Arezastis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 E3 Meter Arezastis

name: Meter Plastena, T.
description: A large, rock-cut Hittite monument located on Mount Sipylus at the locality of Akpınar. The relief depicts a seated figure who is positioned in a niche.

name: Mila Mergi
description: The mountain now called Mila Mergi was called Ilimeru in Assyrian texts of the 8th century B.C. The identification is provided by an Assyrian rock relief dating to the reign of Tiglath-pileser III. The mountain is located ca. 25 kilometers northwest of Dohuk in northern Iraq.

name: Minar-i Chakri
description: Minar-i Chakri, located in the Kabul province of Afghanistan, was the site of a Kushan-era pillar dating to the second or third century AD that was destroyed in the later twentieth century by the Soviet army.

name: Monte Barro
description: Archaeological fieldwork conducted on Monte Barro between 1986 and 1997 produced important data related to the early Middle Ages in northern Italy. A settlement with a military nature develops from the fifth century CE onward. Of particular interest is a structure dubbed "Grande edificio", interpreted as the likely residence of a public official.

name: Monte d'Accoddi
description: A ramped, megalithic structure in Sardinia dated to around 2700-2000 BC.

name: Monument at Ain bou Merzoug
description: Unlabeled feature captured from the Barrington Atlas

name: Monument of Eumenes II/Agrippa
description: A tall plinth next on the left side of the entrance to the Acropolis of Athens, which supported a bronze quadriga. It was probably originally erected for the Attalid king Eumenes II ca. 178 BC, possibly re-dedicated to Marcus Antonius in the 30s BC, and certainly to Agrippa 27-12 BC.

name: Monument of the Eponymous Heroes
description: A marble monument surrounded by a peribolos on the west side of the Athenian Agora, built ca. 350 BC, which supported bronze statues of the namesakes of the Athenian tribes (phylai): Erechtheus, Aigeus, Pandion, Leos, Akamas, Oineus, Kekrops, Hippothoon, Aias/Ajax, and Antiochos. Antigonos Monophthalmos and Demetrios Poliorketes were added in 308/7 BC and removed in 201/0 BC. Ptolemy III was added in 224/3 BC, Attalos I in 201/0 BC, and Hadrian around A.D. 125. The monument was used as the official noticeboard of the Athenian democracy.

name: Monumental entrance arch at Mérida
description: The so-called "Arch of Trajan".

name: Moulis
description: A Gallo-Roman mole that, in 1965, was preserved to a height of 6.4 meters.

name: Mount Judi
description: Mount Judi (also al-Ǧūdiyy, Cûdî‎, Cudi), also spelled Guti and Kutu, is a mountain where Sennacherib had several monuments carved. The Neo-Assyrian name is Mount Nipur.

name: Nahr al-Kalb monuments
description: Over twenty rock reliefs (with accompanying inscriptions) are carved into the limestone cliffs around the estuary of the Nahr al-Kalb river.

name: Naqsh-i Rajab
description: An archaeological site with rock-cut reliefs made under the Sasanian kings Ardašir I (224-241) and Shapur I (241-272).

name: Nashteban
description: Place for Urartian rock inscription.

name: Naveta of Torre Llisà
description: A naviform temple located on Minorca.

name: Nebuchadnezzar II Inscriptions near Brissa
description: Two rock reliefs with inscriptions of the Neo-Babylonian king Nebuchadnezzar II were engraved in the Wadi esh-Sharbin, about 2 km southwest of the modern Lebanese town Brissa (also called Braïssé, Brisa).

name: Nemea
description: Nemea, located north of Argos, was the site of the Panhellenic Games and the Sanctuary of Nemean Zeus.

name: Nemrut Dağ
description: A major sanctuary of Coammagene and site of the mausoleum of Antiochus I (69–34 B.C.). A UNESCO World Heritage Site located on modern Mount Nemrut in Turkey's Adıyaman province.

name: Newgrange
description: Newgrange is a prehistoric monument in County Meath, Ireland, built ca. 3200 BC.

name: Ninfeo della Lucchina
description: A nymphaeum of the third century CE that was identified in 1990 and was likely one component of a larger villa complex in the north-west suburbium of imperial Rome.

name: Nuraghe di Tanca Manna
description: A monopteral nuraghe located near Nuoro, Sardinia.

name: Nymphaeum divi Alexandri
description: A monumental nymphaeum on the Esquiline Hill dating to the Severan period; commonly known as "Trofei di Mario". The complex is located at a fork between the Via Tiburtina and the Via Labicana.

name: Obelisk of Theodosius
description: The Obelisk of Theodosius is an ancient Egyptian obelisk that was originally erected by Pharaoh Thutmose III (1479–1425 BC) to the south of the seventh pylon of the great temple of Karnak. The emperor Theodosius I had the obelisk relocated to Constantinople after AD 390 and it was subsequently re-erected on a newly commissioned sculpted base on the spina of the city's hippodrome.

name: Odeon at Aphrodisias
description: An ancient building at Aphrodisias that is referred to both as a bouleuterion and an odeion.

name: Ojasar-Ilandağ
description: Place for Urartian rock inscription.

name: Old Bouleuterion/Metroon
description: The Old Bouleuterion on the west side of the Agora in Athens was built in the early fifth century BC as the meeting place of the Boule or Council of 500. It also housed the Metroon, a shrine to the Mother of the Gods. The Council moved to the New Bouleuterion at the end of the fifth century BC, but the Old Bouleuterion remained in use as an archive and shrine. It was renovated ca. 140 BC and destroyed in the Herulian Sack of 267 AD. In the early 5th century AD, one of the rooms was rebuilt, possibly for use as a synagogue.

name: Ortadamla
description: Urartian rock inscription.

name: Ortakent
description: Place for Urartian rock inscription.

name: Pagania Sanctuary (Aigies, Laconia)
description: A rural sanctuary, probably of Artemis, located about a kilometer north-northwest of modern Aigies in Laconia. It is surely to be associated with ancient Aigiai, which was located less than two kilometers to the southwest.

name: Paikuli
description: At Paikuli, located in what is now the southern part of Iraqi Kurdistan near the modern-day village of Barkal, Sulaymaniyah Governorate, Iraq, are the remains of a stone masonry tower that was erected as a monument to a victory enjoyed by the Sasanian emperor Narseh over his grandnephew. The inscription is now housed in the Sulaymaniyah Museum.

name: Palatial House at Rhodes
description: A building at the foot of the Acropolis at Rhodes that occupied an entire insula and contained luxurious reception rooms and a large bath suite

name: Palu
description: Fortress with Urartian inscription.

name: Pelopis Thronus
description: The "Throne of Pelops" is a rock-cut monument located at Yarıkkaya on Mount Sipylus.

name: Pierre Ecrite
description: A monumental Latin inscription of the fifth century AD dedicated by Caius Postumus Dardanus, a praetorian prefect.

name: Plemmyrion
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 G4 Plemmyrion

name: Pompei Tropaea
description: In 71 BC as Pompey returned to Italy from Iberia he set up a monument in the Pyrenees known as the Pompei Tropaea. This monument commemorated the 876 cities Pompey had conquered. Pompey's monument was spoliated in late antiquity and a medieval church and later priory were built on the site. Spoliated in turn, what is left of all these structures now comprises the Sitio arqueológico de Panissars on the French/Spanish border.

name: Pompeii II.3.7 garden entrance
description: Pompeii II, 3, 7 is a garden entrance linked to II, 3, 8 and II, 3,9. II, 3, 8 is identified as a workshop, either the Officina vasaria or caupona of Nicanor. This complex was positioned across the street from the Palaestra.

name: Pompey's pillar
description: A Roman triumphal column in Alexandria, Egypt.

name: Pontikokastro
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 F4 Pontikokastro

name: Porta Borsari
description: A first century A.D. gate at Verona, the point at which the Via Postumia entered the city.

name: Portara on Naxos
description: The Portara is the 26-foot-tall doorway of an unfinished Ionic temple of the Late Archaic Period on the island of Naxos.

name: Porte de Mars
description: The Porte de Mars is a Roman triumphal arch in Reims, France, that dates from the third century A.D.

name: Porte Noire (Besançon)
description: A Gallo-Roman triumphal arch dedicated to Marcus Aurelius.

name: Pozzo Pantaleo
description: The 'Pozzo Pantaleo' is a Roman mausoleum that made use of a cistern of the early Imperial period. In a third phase the site was repurposed as a shrine dedicated to the Christian Saint Pantaleon.

name: Propylaea at Athens
description: A monumental gateway at the western end of the Athenian Acropolis. As an element of the Periklean remaking of the Athenian Acropolis, work on the Propylaea commenced in 437 BCE and was abandoned, unfinished, in 432 BCE. The architect Mnesikles was responsible for the Propylaea.

name: Quseir Amra
description: A desert castle built in the early eighth century CE by Walid Ibn Yazid, who would become the future Umayyad caliph Walid II. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1985.

name: Ring of Brodgar
description: The Ring of Brodgar (also known as Brogar, or the Ring o' Brodgar) is a Neolithic henge and stone circle monument in Orkney, Scotland.

name: Rochemaure milestone
description: A Roman milestone of the second century A.D.

name: Rognes
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 15 E2 Rognes

name: Rollright Stones
description: A complex of three Neolithic and Bronze Age megalithic monuments dating from the fourth to second millennia B.C. located on the borders of Oxfordshire and Warwickshire in the English Midlands.

name: Roman Agora of Athens
description: The Roman agora at Athens is located to the north of the Acropolis and to the east of the ancient Agora.

name: Roman arch
description: A triumphal arch located in the civic forum.

name: Roman arch
description: The Roman arch that stands at Musti (Tunisia).

name: Roman arch (Medinaceli)
description: A triple-bay Roman Imperial arch near Medinaceli, Spain, that was originally dedicated to the emperor Domitian and later re-dedicated to Trajan.

name: Roman arch at Capera
description: A quadrifons arch at Capera.

name: Roman arch at Spoletium
description: The so-called "Arch of Drusus and Germanicus" at Spoleto.

name: Roman baths at Is Bangius
description: Terme Romane di Is Bangius

name: Roman forum at Cupra Maritima
description: The Roman forum at Cupra Maritima.

name: Roman milestone at Locmeltro
description: A Roman milestone.

name: Roman villa of Torre di Pordenone
description: A Roman villa and bath complex located in the valley of the Noncello River. The villa complex, whose original construction dates to the first century BCE, was likely rebuilt during the second century CE.

name: Rujm el-Hiri
description: An ancient megalithic structure located in the Israeli-occupied portion of the Golan Heights about 16 km (9.9 miles) east of the coast of the Sea of Galilee. Rujm el-Hiri consists of concentric stone circles with a tumulus at its center. The structure dates to the Early Bronze Age II period (ca. 3000–2700 BCE).

name: Saint-Aunès milestone
description: A Roman milestone of the Via Aurelia.

name: Sanctuary at San Casciano dei Bagni
description: An ancient sanctuary located near the village of San Casciano dei Bagni, located south of Siena, Italy, has produced, following archaeological excavation, a large cache of metallic votive offerings - coins, figurines, statues, anatomical votives - dating to the late republican and early imperial periods. These votives were recovered from waterlogged mud deposits associated with the thermal springs at the site that have been frequented since at least the first millennium BCE.

name: Sanctuary of Artemis at Avlonas
description: A sanctuary possibly sacred to Artemis on the island of Lemnos.

name: Sanctuary of Bendis
description: Sanctuary of Bendis located on the Acropolis of Salamis

name: Sanctuary of Isis and Magna Mater
description: The Sanctuary of Isis and Magna Mater at Roman Mogontiacum (modern Mainz, Germany) was active between the first century and the third century A.D.

name: Sanctuary of Poseidon (Onchestos)
description: Pausanias describes a Sanctuary of Poseidon located at Onchestos that preserved a grove sacred to the god and that Homer had once praised.

name: Sar Mashhad
description: Sar Mashhad, located in the Dadin Rural District, Jereh and Baladeh District, Kazerun County, Fars Province, Iran, is the site of a Sasanian rock relief of a lion hunt, along with accompanying inscriptions. The monument dates to the reign of the king Bahram II (r. 274–293 CE).

name: Septizonium
description: The Septizonium (or Septizodium) is a monument located at the extreme southeastern corner of the Palatine Hill, built by the Severan emperors. It is attested to textually in the Historia Augusta, its footprint is visible on the Severan marble plan (Forma Urbis Romae), and its foundations have been explored through excavation.

name: Serapeum of Alexandria
description: The Serapeum in Alexandria was a temple to Serapis erected by Ptolemy III in the third century B.C. It was destroyed in A.D. 391.

name: Serpent Column
description: An ancient bronze column that was displayed in the Hippodrome of Constantinople, the Serpent Column was part of a monument originally installed at Delphi and relocated to Constantinople by Constantine I in A.D. 324. The monument was originally built to celebrate the Greek defeat of the Persian Empire at the Battle of Plataea in 479 B.C.

name: Shir as-Sanam Inscription
description: A Neo-Babylonian rock relief of Nebuchadnezzar II was found in the area of the modern city Qubbayat, close to the Wadi Oustane and the Jebel Akroum. The monument at Shir as-Sanam is about 16 Km from Qubbayat, on the road to Chambouq and Hermel. It is about located ca. 120 meters from the road, on the western side, 15 m. above the ground.

name: Sillian milestone
description: An uninscribed Roman milestone discovered in 1970 at Sillian, Tirol, Austria. The milestone corresponds to the 24th mile from Aguntum.

name: Slonta
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 C1 Slonta

name: so-called 'Midas Monument'
description: A rock-cut Phrygian monument dating to the seventh century BCE that carries an inscribed dedication in Old Phrygian by Ates, son of Arkias, to Midas.

name: So-called Arch of Hadrian (Capua)
description: The remains of a triple arch in opus latericium marking the pomerium of Capua. It is perhaps originally constructed under the Flavians.

name: So-called Arco di Druso
description: The popular toponym associated with this monument - Arco di Druso or Arch of Drusus - is a misnomer and is misleading. This is problematic given other textual, epigraphic, and archaeological attestations that mention honorific monuments dedicated to Nero Claudius Drusus. This place is the site of the remains of a triple-bay arch that is now reduced to the central bay. The monument sits astride the Via Appia just inside the Porta S. Sebastiano and has been referred to as "Arco di Druso" since the sixteenth century. The monument is built of travertine and faced with marble. This arch, in fact, supported the Aqua Antoniniana, a branch of the Aqua Marcia. The connection with Drusus is erroneous.

name: So-called Roman Gymnasium at Syracuse
description: A monumental complex in the ancient city of Syracuse, probably built around the first century AD. It was initially identified as a gymnasium or a thermal bath; it includes the ruins of a theatre, a quadriporticus, and a temple.

name: Sororium Tigillum
description: Sororium Tigillum describes a wooden beam that stretched across a branch of the Sacra Via that climbed the Oppian Hill near the Compitum Acilii. The monument was flanked by altars of Iuno Sororia and Iuno Curiatius. It is an ancient monument about which there are many stories, the primary one being a link to the Horatii and Curiatii and Publius Horatius' slaying of his own sister.

name: Standing Stones of Stenness
description: The Standing Stones of Stenness is a Neolithic monument on the mainland of Orkney, Scotland, generally dated to the third millennium BC.

name: Statues of Harmodios and Aristogeiton
description: Statues of the tyrannicides Harmodios and Aristogeiton, the assassins of the tyrant Hipparchos and symbols of the Athenian democracy. An initial pair, made by Antenor were erected in the Athenian Agora ca. 510 BC, taken away to Susa by the Persians in 480 BC and returned by Alexander or one of his successors in the late fourth century BC. A replacement pair, made by Kritios and Nesiotes were erected in the Athenian Agora in 477 BC. With few exceptions it was forbidden to erect other statues in the vicinity. Part of the base of the second pair has been found, but not in situ.

name: Stele of Caius Vibius Quartus
description: The stele of a Roman military officer dating to the first century A.D. located at modern Dikilí Tas (Ντικιλί Τας), an archaeological site with evidence for both prehistoric and Byzantine occupation. The stele stands just to the east of modern Krenides, only 2km from the center of the ancient city of Philippi, which lies on the west side of the modern town.

name: Stele Ponte di Apollo
description: A stele carrying a Latin inscription that dates to the Severan period (ca. 206 CE) recording the restoration of the bridge that had been destroyed by the "fury of the sea".

name: Stone of the Pregnant Woman
description: The Stone of the Pregnant Woman in Baalbek, Lebanon is one of the largest monoliths ever quarried.

name: Stonehenge
description: An ancient formation of stones set in a field near Salisbury (Wiltshire County, England).

name: Strassen Roman Milestone
description: A Roman milestone for the twentieth mile from Aguntum was found at modern Strassen in Austria.

name: Surtiĭn dėnzh
description: Ritual complex in central Mongolia consisting of eight deer stone monuments surrounded by nine stone rings and several cairns.

name: Sykeon Roman bath (Thessaly)
description: The remains of a Roman bath complex that included mosaic pavements and marble statuary were documented during rescue excavations in 2004 to the west of Sykeon in Thessaly.

name: Tabula Traiana
description: The Tabula Traiana is a monumental inscription in what is now Serbia commemorating the completion of a Trajanic road along the Danube. The inscription was moved from its original spot to its present location in 1972.

name: Tantalus Monu.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 F4 Tantalus Monu.

name: Taq-i Bustan
description: Site in western Iran with a series of Sassanid rock reliefs carved in the fourth century A.D.

name: Taq-i Girra
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 B2 Taq-i Girra

name: Tell Umm Amer
description: The archaeological site of Tell Umm Amer in the Gaza Strip is included on the Madaba Map under the name "Tabatha". The site was the birthplace in 291 CE of Saint Hilarion who founded an eponymous monastery there. The monastery was destroyed in 614.

name: Temenos of Athena
description: The area on the northeast side of the Piana di Himera is delimited by walls and contains four religious buildings (temples A, B, C, and D), probably dedicated to Athena. The foundations are still visible in the archaeological site of Himera.

name: Tempio di Venere, Baiae
description: The so-called "Tempio di Venere" is likely part of a bathing complex dating to the reign of Hadrian. The surviving structure has an octagonal plan.

name: Temple of Minerva Medica
description: The so-called Temple of Minerva Medica is, in fact, a nymphaeum located along the line of the Anio Vetus aqueduct; it most likely dates to the fourth century AD.

name: Temple of Olympian Zeus, Athens
description: The Temple of Olympian Zeus is also known as the Olympieion. A colossal temple dedicated to Zeus, it was built in three stages, beginning in 515 BCE and ending in 131 CE.

name: Temple of Tellus (Thugga)
description: The Temple of Tellus (Thugga) dates to the reign of Gallienus ca. 261 CE.

name: Temple of the Fabri Navales
description: The Temple of the Ship Carpenters at Ostia dates to the later second century CE.

name: Templum Pacis
description: The Temple of Peace in Rome was vowed by Vespasian following the Jewish War.

name: Tetrapylon at Aphrodisias
description: The monumental columnar entrance to the sanctuary of Aphrodite

name: The Gordian Palace
description: A large Roman Peristyle House with interior gardens, pools, and baths.

name: The Great Sphinx
description: A monumental limestone sphinx situated on the Giza plateau, likely connected with the reign of Pharaoh Khafre.

name: Theater of Icarion Aegeidas (Attica)
description: A small deme theater south of the Agora.

name: Theater of Nero
description: The Theater of Nero located in the Vaticanus Ager is relatively little known and receives little attention in the ancient sources. Archaeological discoveries announced in the summer of 2023 seem to point to remains of the theater preserved beneath the courtyard of the Palazzo della Rovere located between the Borgo Santo Spirito and the Via della Conciliazione. The first-century CE theater seems to have been dismantled by the second-century CE.

name: Theatrum Marcelli
description: Theatrum Marcelli (Theater of Marcellus) is an ancient Roman theater in the Circus Flaminius. Augustus completed its construction and dedicated it in 12 BC.

name: Torre degli Embrici
description: An imperial Roman villa with a bath complex that emerged in the first and second centuries AD, transitioning to a fortified site after the sixth century AD.

name: Torre del Filosofo
description: The so-called "Torre del Filosofo" was a structure on Mt. Etna that may have been built by the Roman emperor Hadrian in honor of the Greek philosopher Empedocles as local legend held that he once climbed the mountain and died by falling into the volcano.

name: Torre del Monumento
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 C4 Torre del Monumento

name: Tourraque d'Ortolas
description: A Gallo-Roman tower.

name: Tower of the Winds
description: Also known as the Horologion of Andronikos Kyrrhestes, the Tower of the Winds is an octagonal Pentelic marble clocktower located in the "Roman" Agora that functioned as a horologion.

name: Triumphal Arch of Glanum
description: A Roman triumphal arch built in the early first century A.D.

name: Triumphal Arch of Orange
description: A triumphal arch most likely built during the reign of Augustus to honor veterans of the Gallic Wars and Legio II Augusta.

name: Tropaeum Augusti/Alpium Tropaeum/Alpis Summa/Alpis Maritima
description: A victory monument built by Augustus (5 B.C.) to commemorate the subjugation of the tribes of the western Alps. The Romans considered the spot to be "In Alpe Maritima".

name: Tropaeum Traiani (monument)
description: A war memorial near the ancient settlement of Tropaeum Traiani (modern Adamclisi)

name: Troulli tis Korfis
description: A hilltop sanctuary, in use from the Bronze Age until the time of the Roman Empire.

name: Tsimintiri Archaic Site
description: The small Aegean island of Tsimintiri, located between Despotiko and Antiparos, has produced archaeological evidence for a large sanctuary and/or other public complex of archaic date, likely to be associated with the extensive archaic sanctuary of Mandra, located on the island of Despotiko, to which Tsimintiri was connected in antiquity.

name: Uffington White Horse
description: The Uffington White Horse is stylized prehistoric hill figure dating to the later prehistoric period in England.

name: Uley
description: At West Hill, Uley, Gloucestershire, excavations revealed the remains of a multi-period ritual site. This site remained in use from the Neolithic period to the Middle Ages. An Iron Age sanctuary was replaced by a Romano-British temple dedicated to Mercury in the early second century A.D.

name: Umbilicus urbis Romae
description: The remains of a monument marking the symbolic navel of the city of Rome that dates to the reign of Septimius Severus.

name: Unnamed rock relief
description: An unnamed Persian rock relief located near Sundurlu.

description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 D4 unnamed unlabeled ((7 monuments, 1 cemetery, 2 walls))

name: Uragh Stone Circle
description: A stone circle measuring 33 by 26 feet with 11 standing stones located near Gleninchaquin Park, Tuosist, County Kerry, Ireland.

name: Valcamonica
description: Located in the Lombardy plain, the Valcamonica assemblage constitutes one of the world's greatest collections of prehistoric petroglyphs – created over a period of 8,000 years. The site was enlisted by UNESCO in 1971.

name: Vatican Obelisk
description: An obelisk from Heliopolis in Egypt that the emperor Caius erected in the Circus Gaii et Neronis. It now stands in St. Peter's square in Vatican City, having been re-erected there by Domenico Fontana in 1586.

name: Ventimiglia Roman Theater
description: A Roman theater at modern Ventimiglia in Italy, dating to the early second century CE.

name: Vessagiriya
description: Vessagiriya is the site of an ancient Buddhist forest monastery in Sri Lanka. It is considered part of the ruined site of Anuradhapura. The monastery was initially built in the middle of the third century BCE and was expanded in the late fifth century CE. A Roman bronze coin, as well as punch-marked coins and/or ingots, were excavated at Vessagiriya in the first decade of the 20th century.

name: Villa delle Guardiole
description: A Late Republican to early Imperial coastal villa located at the mouth of the Fosso delle Guardiole near Castrum Novum. Two artificial fish ponds are associated with the villa.

name: Villa Imperiale of Milan
description: A palatial Roman villa dating to the reign of Maximian.

name: Vounari
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 D2 Vounari

name: Wadi es-Saba Monuments
description: Two inscribed reliefs of a Neo-Babylonian king (probably Nebuchadnezzar II) are carved on a rock face in the Wadi es-Saba (“Valley of the Lion”), southwest of Akroum, Lebanon.

name: Wilson's Arch
description: Stone archway constructed as part of the expansion of the Temple Mount in Jerusalem during the early Roman period.

name: Yaat
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 B5 Yaat

name: Yazılıkaya (Eskişehir)
description: Yazılıkaya (also known as 'Midas City') is an archaeological site in Eskişehir Province, Turkey, known for its Phrygian rock-cut monuments, facades, altars, and niches dedicated to the Mother Goddess Matar.

name: Yoncalı
description: Yoncalı is located near the modern cities of Malazgirt and Bulanık, at the western end of the Bulanık-Malazgirt plain. The Assyrian king Tiglat-pileser I placed an inscribed, rock-cut relief here to commemorate his victories in the region.

name: Yukarı Anzaf
description: Site for Urartian palace and citadel.

name: Zvartnots Cathedral
description: A seventh century AD church built by Catholicos Nerses the Builder (AD 641-653) and located in the Armavir Province of Armenia. The ruins of the church were listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2000.

name: 'Ain el-Sabil Church
description: Late antique mud-brick church located at Ain el-Sabil in Egypt. The exact dating of the site is still unknown.

name: 'Evron
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 B4 'Evron

name: ‘Trebula’
description: A pre-Roman site in the valley of the Sangro river that was later converted to a Christian church.

name: "Basilica" of San Vitale
description: A church (but not a basilica) in Ravenna, Italy, established by Bishop Ecclesius in 526 AD and consecrated ca. 547 AD.

name: "The Great Basilica" at Butrint
description: The so-called "Great Basilica" is an early Christian basilica dating to the sixth century.

name: [Hagios Prokopios]
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 63 F3 [Hagios Prokopios]

name: *Chrononum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 I3 *Chrononum

name: *Iciacum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 I3 *Iciacum

name: *Manatum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 H2 *Manatum

name: *Marciacum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 I3 *Marciacum

name: *Melita
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 H3 *Melita

name: *Novivicum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 F1 *Novivicum

name: *Pauliacum, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 G2 *Pauliacum, Mon.

name: *Qubba
description: A monastery constructed in the fifth century CE on the site of a cave previously inhabited by St. Paul the Anchorite.

name: *Randanum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 I3 *Randanum

name: *Siroialum?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 F3 *Siroialum?

name: Abbazia di San Vincenzo al Volturno
description: An historic Benedictine monastery located in the Province of Isernia, near the source of the river Volturno.

name: Abbey of Monte Cassino
description: A multi-period hilltop settlement with evidence for ancient terrace construction that now supports an abbey of the Benedictine order.

name: Abbeyknockmoy abbey
description: A ruined Medieval Cistercian abbey founded in 1190.

name: Ad Herculem
description: An ancient station on the Roman road to Gades recorded in the Antonine Itinerary, ad Herculem must have been associated with the Herakleion on the southern end of the island of Cotinussa. Scholars differ on the precise location; their efforts are complicated by significant landscape change since antiquity.

name: Ag. Dimitrios
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 inset Ag. Dimitrios

name: Ag. Isidoros
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 C3 Ag. Isidoros

name: Ag. Lot
description: The Sanctuary of Agios Lot is located at the southeastern end of the Dead Sea.

name: Ag. Mavri
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 B3 Ag. Mavri

name: Ag. Panagia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 D2 Ag. Panagia

name: Ag. Sophia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 C4 Ag. Sophia

name: Ag. Stephanos
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 B3 Ag. Stephanos

name: Ag. Thomas
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 C4 Ag. Thomas

name: Ag. Trias
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 E1 Ag. Trias

name: Agia Dynamis
description: A sixteenth-century chapel in Athens built atop an ancient shrine of Herakles.

name: Ain el Aouad
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 E2 Ain el Aouad

name: Ain Nagila
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 D4 Ain Nagila

name: Akoimeton Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 53 B2 Akoimeton Mon.

name: al-Husn Church
description: A ruined and now largely dispersed fifth-century CE church on the outskirts of al-Bara, Syria.

name: Alexandros, Mon.
description: Monastery founded by  Alexander the Acoemete in 782 CE on the left bank of the Euphrates, near the modern village of Halawa.

name: Ampass early Christian Church
description: The high altar of the present, Gothic-era Pfarrkirche (parish church) of modern Ampass (near Insbruck in Austria) was built over a series of earlier Christian churches, the earliest of which appears to date to the fifth century CE, based on excavated remains of a reliquary vault dating back to that period.

name: Amphitheater of Durrës
description: The Roman amphitheater of Durrës dates to the reign of Trajan. A small chapel built inside the amphitheater has mosaics likely dating to the 6th-8th centuries CE.

name: Apa Abraham, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 G4 Apa Abraham, Mon.

name: Apa Apollo
description: A Coptic monastery founded ca. 385/390 located 80 km north of Asyut, near the village of Dashlout, Egypt.

name: Apa Apollo, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 E3 Apa Apollo, Mon.

name: Apa Bane, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 D1 Apa Bane, Mon.

name: Apa Djoore, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 F3 Apa Djoore, Mon.

name: Apa Epiphanius, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 80 B2 Apa Epiphanius, Mon.

name: Apa Ioannes, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 E2 Apa Ioannes, Mon.

name: Apa Makrobios, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 E3 Apa Makrobios, Mon.

name: Apa Palemon, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 G4 Apa Palemon, Mon.

name: Apa Psoi, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 F3 Apa Psoi, Mon.

name: Apa Psote, Mon./Amba Bisada
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 F4 Apa Psote, Mon./Amba Bisada

name: Apa Senouthou (monastery)
description: The "White Monastery", located near modern Tahta and Sohag in Egypt.

name: Apa Souroutos, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 E3 Apa Souroutos, Mon.

name: Apollo Asgelatos (temple)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 B5 Apollo Asgelatos, T.

name: Apollo, T.
description: Two early Christian basilicae were superimposed over the ancient sanctuary of Apollo Dalios.

name: Apostoli, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 D2 Apostoli, Mon.

name: Aqaba Church
description: A pre-Constantinian Christian church located at ancient Aila in Jordan.

name: Arachnaion (mountain)
description: A mountain of the Argolid with a higher western peak (Profitis Ilias, 1199 masl) and a lower eastern peak (Mavrovouni or Arna, 1139 masl). Material from the Mycenaean, Archaic, and Classical periods has been found on the western peak and identified with altars of Zeus and Hera described by Pausanias. Aeschylus mentions its use as a signal station.

name: Archaic Temple at Ai Lia
description: Badly damaged foundations of the peristyle of a large Doric temple of the 6th or early 5th c. BCE, the center of which is now occupied by the modern chapel of Profitis Ilias on a rise NE of ancient Kyreneia. This temple was probably destroyed during the earthquake of 373 BCE and replaced by a smaller temple to the west, built from its rubble. A monumental great altar has also been excavated immediately east of the temple.

name: Archbishop's Chapel, Ravenna
description: A private, early Christian oratory in Ravenna, Italy, erected by Peter II as archbishop in A.D. 495.

name: Arian Baptistry
description: Baptistry built by Theodoric the Great in the late fifth or early sixth century AD

name: Asabaa
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 E2 Asabaa

name: Aula Palatina
description: The Aula Palatina is a Roman Imperial Basilica of the emperor Constantine (German: Konstantinbasilika) located at Trier, Germany. It was built ca. A.D. 310 as part of a Roman palace complex.

name: Ayios Filon
description: A ruined fifth-century CE basilica on the Karpass peninsula of Cyprus.

name: Baldersdorf
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 F3 Baldersdorf

name: Baptistery of Butrint
description: The Baptistery of Butrint is a round structure, with a diameter of 14.5 meters. It was built in the sixth century A.D. and was excavated in May 1928 by Luigi Maria Ugolini.

name: Baptistry of Neon
description: An octagonal brick structure erected by Bishop Ursus (late fourth or early fifth century AD) as part of a Basilica (destroyed in 1734). Bishop Neon completed the baptistry at the end of the fifth century AD.

name: Barlaamus
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 A2 Barlaamus

name: Basilica
description: An important civic building in Pompeii and an early example of the building type, built between ca. 120 and 78 B.C. (VIII, 1, 1).

name: Basilica Aemilia
description: A basilica located at the juncture of the Sacra Via and the Argiletum flanking the Forum Romanum that was initially built in 179 B.C.E.

name: Basilica and convent of S. Ippolito
description: An important paleochristian complex located in the area of Portus.

name: Basilica at Aspendos
description: The remains of a Roman Basilica at Aspendos.

name: Basilica at Saepinum
description: The Basilica at Saepinum is positioned at the intersection of the cardo and the decumanus.

name: Basilica Benedettina di S. Michele Arcangelo
description: An abbey church located in Capua on the western slopes of Monte Tifata.

name: Basílica Casa Herrera
description: The Casa Herrera Basilica is part of the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Archaeological Ensemble of Mérida". The paleochristian basilica was built in either the late fifth or early sixth century CE.

name: Basilica Constantini
description: The Basilica Constantini, also referred to as Basilica Nova ("New Basilica"), Basilica of Maxentius or Basilica of Maxentius and Constantine, is a colossal fourth-century basilica located along the Sacra Via in Rome. It is considered the largest ancient structure in the valley of the Forum Romanum and is typically deemed the final Roman civic basilica built in the ancient city.

name: Basilica Damasiana
description: The basilica and oratory of Damasianus, as well as the martyr tombs of Simplicius, Faustinus, Beatrice, and Ruinianus.

name: Basilica Damous el-Karita
description: A Christian basilica of the Byzantine period located in Carthage.

name: Basílica de Santa Eulalia
description: The Basílica de Santa Eulalia is a paleochristian site that is included as part of the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Archaeological Ensemble of Mérida". The early Christian features were superimposed upon previous, preexisting Roman features.

name: Basílica de Son Peretó
description: A Paleochristian site dating to the fifth century A.D.

name: Basilica di San Marco
description: The ruins of a Christian basilica located near the Via Ardeatina on the grounds of the Calixtian complex have been connected with the Basilica of Saint Mark. The site was discovered accidentally in 1991 and is associated with the Catacomb of Balbina.

name: Basilica di Sant'Agnese fuori le mura
description: Pope Honorius I commissions the Basilica of Saint Agnes in the seventh century, replacing the earlier Constantinian structure.

name: Basilica di Santa Balbina all'Aventino
description: The basilica of Santa Balbina is a paleo-Christian basilica in Rome, located on the "little Aventine".

name: Basilica di Santa Sinforosa
description: A minor Paleochristian basilica located in Guidonia Montecelio that was dedicated to the patron saint of Tivoli.

name: Basilica di Santo Stefano in via Latina
description: The now-ruined basilica of Santo Stefano in Via Latina was a fifth-century CE early Christian basilica. It is now located within the confines of the archaeological park of the Via Latina tombs.

name: Basilica Hilariana
description: A basilica erected on the Caelian Hill in Rome under the patronage of Manius Publicus Hilarus and connected to the cult of Cybele and Attis.

name: Basilica Manastirine
description: An important extra-mural Christian site at Solina. The site includes the basilica and the cemetery of Manastirine, associated with a Christian martyr.

name: Basilica Neptuni
description: A building in the Campus Martius that originally dates to ca. 25 BCE.

name: Basilica of Campanopetra
description: The ruins of a three-aisled basilica of the sixth century CE located at Salamis in Cyprus.

name: Basilica of Carsulae
description: The civic Basilica of Carsulae is located on the forum.

name: Basilica of Constantine (Ostia)
description: The presence of a basilica created with funds donated by Constantine I in region 5 of ancient Ostia was known from textual sources and then confirmed via geophysical prospection and, ultimately, excavation.

name: Basilica of Cresconius
description: The Basilica of Cresconius is a church with a nave-and-side-aisle plan located at Cuicul (Djémila), Numidia, Algeria.

name: Basilica of Hildeguns
description: A basilica dating to the time of the Vandals.

name: Basilica of Iomnium
description: The basilica of Iomnium (Tigzirt) likely dates to the fifth and sixth centuries CE.

name: Basilica of Lechaion
