name: Roman amphitheater of Beneventum
description: The Roman amphitheater of Beneventum dates to the reign of the emperor Nero.

name: Roman amphitheater of Carthage
description: A Roman amphitheater built at Carthage.

name: Roman amphitheater of Catania
description: The Roman amphitheater of Catania is the largest known ancient amphitheater in Sicily. Its external dimensions measure ca. 125 x 105 meters. It likely dates to the second century.

name: Roman amphitheater of Col. Tarraco
description: A Roman amphitheater in Tarragona built in the first century A.D. The structure was renovated under Elagabalus.

name: Roman amphitheater of Italica
description: The amphitheater of Italica, built in the second century A.D., was one of the largest in the Roman world and could accommodate an estimated 25,000 spectators.

name: Roman amphitheater of Martigny
description: The Roman amphitheater of Martigny likely dates to the reign of Trajan.

name: Roman amphitheater of Milan
description: The Roman amphitheater of Milan was built near the Porta Ticinese during the second and third centuries. It was destroyed by the time of the Gothic Wars, most likely during the fifth century.

name: Roman amphitheater of Saintes
description: The Gallo-Roman amphitheater of Mediolanum Santonum.

name: Roman Amphitheater of Serdica
description: The Roman Amphitheater of Serdica was built during the third and fourth centuries CE atop remains of an earlier theater complex. The complex was abandoned by the fifth century CE. The site was rediscovered beginning in 2004.

name: Roman amphitheater of Thyna
description: A Roman amphitheater located at Thaenea, a Roman station in the province of Africa Proconsularis.

name: Roman amphitheater of Tours
description: A Roman amphitheater at Tours that dates to the first century CE.

name: Roman amphitheater of Venusia
description: A Roman amphitheater dating to the first and second centuries.

name: Roman amphitheater of Virunum
description: The Virunum Roman amphitheater is located in Maria Saal, Austria.

name: Roman amphitheater, Córdoba
description: The Roman amphitheater of Córdoba.

name: Roman amphitheatre, Charterhouse
description: An amphitheater stood to the west of the lead mining settlement at Charterhouse Roman Town.

name: Roman civil amphitheater at Carnuntum
description: Located outside the city wall, the Roman amphitheater at Carnuntum dates to the later second century. It could accommodate some 13,000 spectators. The amphitheater likely went out of use by the fourth century.

name: Roman macellum at Aeclanum
description: Once hypothetically identified by some scholars as an amphitheater, this structure at Aeclanum is now understood to be a Roman macellum.

name: Roman Odeion at Corinth
description: The Roman Odeion at Corinth dates to the first century A.D.

name: Roman odeon, Vienne
description: The Roman odeon at Vienne.

name: Roman theater
description: The theater at Thugga dates to 168 or 169 and could accommodate 3500 spectators.

name: Roman theater (Haidra)
description: The remains of a Roman theater located at Haidra, Tunisia.

name: Roman theater at [Augusta] Ambianorum
description: The Roman theater lies to the east of the ancient settlement.

name: Roman theater at Aizanoi
description: The Roman theater at Aizanoi is part of a theater-stadium complex. The complex is built after A.D. 160 with the patronage of one M. Apuleius Eurycles.

name: Roman theater at Amiternum
description: The Roman theater at Amiternum could seat approximately 2,000 people. It was abandoned after the fourth century and became a necropolis.

name: Roman theater at Amman/Philadelpheia
description: A second century Roman theater at Amman that could seat 6,000 spectators.

name: Roman theater at Antium
description: The Roman theater at Antium is most likely built in the second half of the first century.

name: Roman theater at Aosta
description: The Roman theater at Aosta dates ca. 25 B.C.

name: Roman theater at Asculum
description: The Roman theater at Asculum was discovered during excavations in 1932. The structure dates to the first century A.D.

name: Roman theater at Augusta Bagiennorum
description: The Roman theater at Augusta Bagiennorum

name: Roman theater at Benevento
description: A Roman theater of the second century A.D. constructed during the reign of Hadrian.

name: Roman Theater at Bosra
description: The Roman Theater at Bosra dates to the second century A.D.

name: Roman theater at Brixia
description: The Roman theater at Brixia was built during the Flavian period.

name: Roman theater at Cádiz
description: The Roman theater at Cádiz was one of the largest in the Roman world, accommodating some 20,000 spectators. It dates originally to the first century B.C. In the thirteenth century it was incorporated into a fortress built by King Alfonso X of Castile.

name: Roman theater at Cales
description: The Roman theater at Cales dates to the first century B.C.

name: Roman theater at Carsulae
description: The Roman theater at Carsulae dates to the Augustan period, pre-dating the amphitheater.

name: Roman theater at Carthage
description: The Roman theater at Carthage was built around the middle of the second century A.D. It was re-discovered in 1904.

name: Roman theater at Carthago Nova/Col. Urbs Iulia
description: The Roman theater at Cartagena dates to the very end of the first century B.C. and was dedicated to Caius and Lucius Caesar.

name: Roman theater at Casinum
description: The Roman theater at Casinum dates to the Augustan period.

name: Roman theater at Castrum Novum
description: The Roman theater at Castrum Novum (Santa Marinella, Italy).

name: Roman theater at Col. Augusta Raurica
description: The theater at Augusta Raurica is one of the best preserved Roman theaters north of the Alps.

name: Roman theater at Cuicul
description: The Roman theater at Cuicul was built during the reign of Antoninus Pius.

name: Roman theater at Falerii
description: The Roman theater at Falerii was excavated in 1829.

name: Roman theater at Falerio Picenus
description: The Roman theater at Falerio Picenus was completed during the reign of Tiberius.

name: Roman theater at Ferentinum
description: The Roman theater at Ferentinum is today poorly preserved.

name: Roman theater at Ferentium
description: The Roman theater at Ferentium dates to the early Imperial period.

name: Roman theater at Fiesole
description: The Roman theater at Fiesole dates to the first century.

name: Roman theater at Fréjus
description: The Roman theater at Fréjus dates to the reign of Tiberius.

name: Roman theater at Gortyna
description: The Roman theater at Gortyna dates to the second century.

name: Roman theater at Gosbecks
description: A Roman-British theater was excavated some 125 yards due south of the Gosbecks sanctuary.

name: Roman theater at Helikore/Nicaea
description: Pliny the Younger discussed the restoration of the theater in a letter sent to the Roman emperor Trajan.

name: Roman theater at Helvia Ricina
description: Helvia Ricina flourished under Augustus and Tiberius and its theater is the largest in Picenum.

name: Roman theater at Herakleia
description: The Roman theater at Herakleia dates to the time of Hadrian and remained in use until the fourth century. Its remains came to light in 1968.

name: Roman theater at Iguvium
description: The Roman theater at Iguvium is located within the Guastuglia archaeological area and dates to the late republican period. Sear (2004) notes several important architectural innovations evident in the theater's construction.

name: Roman theater at Interamnia Praetuttiorum
description: A Roman theater dating to the Augustan period.

name: Roman theater at Iol Caesarea
description: An important early example of Roman theatral architecture.

name: Roman theater at Italica
description: The Roman theater at Italica could accommodate 3,000 spectators. It predates the Hadrianic rebuilding of the city.

name: Roman theater at Iuvanum
description: The Roman theater at Iuvanum is located at the foot of the acropolis.

name: Roman theater at Lepcis Magna
description: The Roman theater at Lepcis Magna was financed in the first century by Annobal Tapapius Rufus.

name: Roman theater at Libarna
description: The Roman theater at Libarna dates to the first century.

name: Roman theater at Lupia(e)
description: The precise date of the Roman theater at Lecce is uncertain, but it is generally assigned to the Augustan period.

name: Roman theater at Malaca
description: The remains of the first century B.C. Roman theater are located adjacent to the Alcazaba of Málaga. The theater is not completely excavated, although epigraphic data places it in the Augustan period. Its abandonment came in the third century.

name: Roman Theater at Mérida
description: The Roman Theatre of Mérida was sponsored by Marcus Agrippa and dates ca. 16-15 B.C.

name: Roman theater at Metellinum
description: The Roman theater at Metellinum (modern Medelin, Spain) dates to the late first century B.C.

name: Roman theater at Mogontiacum
description: The site of the Roman theater in Mainz.

name: Roman theater at Nicopolis
description: The Roman theater at Nicopolis incorporates both Greek and Roman architectural practices in its construction.

name: Roman theater at Ocriculum
description: The Roman theater at Ocriculum dates to the early imperial period.

name: Roman theater at Ostia
description: The Roman theater at Ostia was built under the patronage of Marcus Agrippa.

name: Roman theater at Pelusium
description: The Roman theater at Pelusium (Tell Farama).

name: Roman theater at Perge
description: The Roman theater at Perge was built ca. 120 AD and renovated ca. 250 AD.

name: Roman theater at Petra
description: The Roman theater at Petra.

name: Roman theater at Salamis/Konstantia
description: Salamis remained an important city of Cyprus during the Roman period and the theater is one part of a complex of monumental architecture there.

name: Roman theater at Salona
description: The Roman theater of Salona.

name: Roman Theater at Sepphoris
description: The ancient Roman Theater at Sepphoris.

name: Roman theater at Soloi/Aipeia?
description: The Roman theater at Soloi, Cyprus.

name: Roman theater at Sparta
description: The Roman theater at Sparta dates to the late first century B.C.

name: Roman theater at Spoletium
description: The Roman theater at Spoletium dates to the first century B.C.

name: Roman theater at Suessa Aurunca
description: The Roman theater at Suessa Aurunca was built in the first century A.D. and then restored under Antoninus Pius. Amedeo Maiuri initiated excavations at the theater site in the 1920s. The excavation was not completed until 2003.

name: Roman theater at Sufetula
description: The Roman theater at Sufetula.

name: Roman theater at Teate
description: The Roman theater at Teate measures 80 meters in diameter.

name: Roman theater at Tergeste
description: The Roman theater at Tergeste dates to the late first century A.D.

name: Roman theater at Terracina
description: The ancient Roman theater of Terracina dates from the first century BCE.

name: Roman theater at Thignica
description: The Roman theater at Thignica.

name: Roman theater at Tlos
description: The Roman theater at Tlos preserves 34 rows of seating.

name: Roman theater at Tusculum
description: The Roman theater at Tusculum was first excavated in 1839.

name: Roman theater at Utica
description: The Roman theater at Utica.

name: Roman theater at Vendeuil-Caply
description: The larger of the two Roman theaters at Vendeuil-Caply was located at the northwest angle of the cardo and the decumanus.

name: Roman theater at Verona
description: The Roman theater at Verona dates from the late first century B.C.

name: Roman theater at Volaterrae
description: The Roman theater of Volaterrae was built during the during the Augustan period under the consulship of Aulus Caecina Severus.

name: Roman theater of Acinipo
description: The Roman theater of Acinipo dates ca. 59-53 B.C.

name: Roman theater of Adraha
description: Ruins of a Roman theater located at modern Dar'a in Syria.

name: Roman theater of Aspendos
description: The Roman theater at Aspendos dates ca. AD 155 and is perhaps the best preserved example of a Roman theater building.

name: Roman Theater of Augustodunum
description: The Roman theater of Augustodunum dates to the reign of Trajan and was dedicated in 105 CE.

name: Roman Theater of Baelo
description: A Roman theater in Baelo that dates to the reign of Claudius.

name: Roman theater of Catania
description: The Roman theater of Catania was built in the second century CE and remained in use until the fifth or sixth century. It could accommodate approximately 7,000 spectators.

name: Roman theater of Fourvière
description: A Roman theater in Lyon, France, that originally dates ca. 15 B.C.

name: Roman theater of Guelma
description: The Roman theater of ancient Calama (now Guelma) was built in the early third century CE and substantially restored in the twentieth century.

name: Roman theater of Herculaneum
description: The Roman theater of Herculaneum was constructed during the Augustan period. The theater was discovered first in 1709 while a well was being dug.

name: Roman Theater of Jableh
description: A Roman theater located at Gabala (Syria).

name: Roman theater of Lokroi Epizephyrioi
description: A Roman theater of the first century B.C. was built on the site of the fourth century B.C. Greek theater.

name: Roman theater of Milan
description: The Roman theater of Milan was built during the Augustan period and could accommodate 7,000 to 9,000 spectators.

name: Roman Theater of Minturnae
description: A theater constructed in Minturnae under Augustus or Tiberius, and further embellished under Hadrian.

name: Roman theater of Neapolis
description: The Roman theater of Neapolis is a structure of the first century B.C. that is built atop the pre-existing fourth century B.C. Greek theater.

name: Roman theater of Philippopolis
description: The Roman theater in Plovdiv, Bulgaria.

name: Roman theater of Privernum
description: The Roman theater of Privernum is attested mainly by means of epigraphic evidence.

name: Roman theater of Saguntum
description: The Roman theater of Saguntum could accommodate approximately 8,000 spectators.

name: Roman theater of Scythopolis
description: The Roman theater of Scythopolis.

name: Roman Theater of Side
description: The Roman Theater of Side dates to the second century CE.

name: Roman theater of Stobi
description: The Roman theater of Stobi dates to the second century.

name: Roman theater of Suasa
description: The Roman theater of Suasa was discovered in 2003 during excavations near the amphitheater. The semicircular structure measures ca. 55 meters in diameter and dates to the first century A.D.

name: Roman theater of Vienne
description: A first century Roman theater in Vienne.

name: Roman theater of Zaragoza
description: Dating to the first century, the Roman theater of Zaragoza accommodated about 6,000 spectators.

name: Roman theater, Atri
description: The Roman theater of Atri.

name: Roman theater, Jublains
description: The Roman theater at modern Jublains.

name: Roman theater, Singilia Barba
description: The Roman theater of Singilia Barba

name: Roman theater, Tipasa
description: The Roman theater at Tipasa.

name: Roman Theatre at Caere
description: The Roman theater at Caere (modern Cerveteri, Italy) is known especially for the sculptural program honoring Julio-Claudian emperors, including Tiberius and Claudius.

name: Roman Theatre of Orange
description: The Roman Theatre of Orange was built early in the first century A.D.

name: Sabratha amphitheater
description: The Sabratha amphitheater dates to the later second century.

name: Sabratha Roman Theater
description: A large Roman theater, likely built sometime in the third century CE.

name: San Roque Theatre - Carteia
description: The Roman theater of Carteia.

name: Sanctuary of Dionysos
description: The Sanctuary of Dionysos, which includes a theater, choregic monument, statue bases, and at least one temple, formed part of the civic center of the Attic deme of Ikarion.

name: Sapperton and Cirencester amphitheatre
description: The Cirencester amphitheater is the second largest known in Britannia. Its earthworks supported tiered seats that could accommodate about 8,000 spectators. The amphitheater was converted to a fortified site in the fifth century during the Saxon invasions.

name: Sarmizegetusa Roman amphitheater
description: The Roman amphitheater at Colonia Ulpia Traiana Augusta Dacica Sarmizegetusa was built in A.D. 108/110 and renovated in 158 under the legio IV Flavia Felix

name: Second ancient theater, Larisa
description: The second ancient theater at Larisa dates to the first century BCE.

name: Simitthu Roman theater
description: The Roman theater at Simitthu, an ancient Roman-Berber town in northwestern Tunisia.

name: Small Roman theater at Vendeuil-Caply
description: Site of the small Roman theater on Le Mont Châtelet.

name: Small Theater (Antipatris)
description: A small theater of the Roman era, located on the site of Antipatris. It may never have been completed, or it was heavily spoliated prior to its ultimate destruction in an earthquake in 363 CE. The original excavator tentatively suggested identifying it as an odeon, but for various archaeological and stylistic reasons, this view has not been widely accepted.

name: So-called Theatre 2
description: The ruins of an ancient theatre in Cyrene, known as "Theatre 2".

name: So-called Theatre 3
description: A Roman theatre on the acropolis ridge of Cyrene, conventionally numbered "3".

name: South Theater (Jerash)
description: The South Theater of Jerash in Jordan dates to the late first century CE.

name: St-André-sur-Cailly
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 B3 St-André-sur-Cailly

name: St-Georges-du-Bois
description: St-Georges-du-Bois is the site of a Gallo-Roman amphitheater.

name: Studforth Hill
description: The remains of a Romano-British amphitheater were discovered on Studforth Hill, just outside the village of Aldborough between Harrogate and York in northern England.

name: Susa Roman amphitheater
description: The Roman amphitheater at Segusio.

name: Tavium Theater
description: The Roman theater of Tavium.

name: Teatro Clunia
description: A Roman theater initially constructed during the reign of Tiberius.

name: Teatro Romano de Regina
description: The Roman theater at Regina, now Badajoz, Spain.

name: Teatro romano, Aquino
description: The Roman theater of Aquinum.

name: The Theater of Larissa
description: The theater at Larissa was a large stone theater built around the end of the third century BC and used for dramatic performances and as a political meeting place.

name: Theater 1 (Telmessos)
description: An excavated ancient theater at Telmessos (Fethiye) has been variously dated to the late Hellenistic or Augustan periods. Some components (e.g., the extant stage structures) date to later in the Roman imperial period.

name: Theater 2 (Telmessos)
description: A small theater, located on the north slope of the acropolis at Telmessos.

name: Theater at Alinda/Alexandria ad Latmum
description: The Roman Theater at Alinda could accommodate about 5,000 spectators.

name: Theater at Arykanda
description: The Theater at Arykanda dates to the first century B.C.

name: Theater at Bodrum
description: The Theater at Bodrum is dedicated to Ares.

name: Theater at Butrint
description: A Greek and Roman theater at the site of Butrint.

name: Theater at Cillium
description: The Roman theater at Cillium.

name: Theater at Cyrrus/Hagioupolis
description: The Roman theater at Cyrrus dates ca. A.D. 100 - 220.

name: Theater at Delphi
description: The theater at Delphi near the Temple of Apollo.

name: Theater at Heraclea Minoa
description: The Theater at Heraclea Minoa dates to the late fourth century B.C.

name: Theater at Kibyra
description: The Theater at Kibyra dates to the Hellenistic period.

name: Theater at Leuke
description: The theater is located at the north-east end of Kouphonesi. It was a stone-built building that could accommodated ca. 1000 spectators.

name: Theater at Madauros
description: The Roman theater at Madauros in Africa Proconsularis.

name: Theater at Patara/Arsinoe
description: The Theater at Patara/Arsinoe dates to the reign of Tiberius and was subsequently renovated under the Antonines.

name: Theater at Pausilipum
description: A small Roman theater with a capacity of approximately 2,000 spectators.

name: Theater at Philippi
description: The initial phase of the Theater at Philippi dates to just after 356 B.C. when Philip II conquered the city.

name: Theater at Phoinike
description: The Greek Theater at Phoinike in Epirus.

name: Theater at Saepinum
description: The intramural theater at Saepinum is one of the best preserved structures at the site. Located in the northern part of the walled area, it is likely built between 2. B.C. and A.D. 4.

name: Theater at Segobriga
description: Construction of the Roman Theater at Segobriga began under Tiberius and was completed during the Flavian period.

name: Theater at Stymphalos
description: The remains of a rock-cut theater at Stymphalos.

name: Theater at Sybaris/Thurii/Copia
description: The Roman Theater at Sybaris/Thurii/Copia dates originally to the Augustan period.

name: Theater at Termessos
description: The Theater at Termessos is a Hellenistic and Roman structure, with the Roman theater preserving elements of the earlier phase.

name: Theater at Termessos
description: The ancient Theater at Termessos.

name: Theater at the Sanctuary of Asklepios, Epidauros
description: The Greek theater in the Sanctuary of Asklepios at Epidauros, renowned for its acoustics

name: Theater at Thorikos
description: The largely preserved classical theater at Thorikos is dated ca. 525-480 B.C.

name: Theater at Xanthos
description: The Greek theater (second century BC) was renovated following a Roman design following the earthquake of A.D. 141.

name: Theater of ancient Sicyon
description: The Theater of ancient Sicyon dates to either the late fourth or early third century BC. It was restructured after 146 B.C.

name: Theater of Aphrodisias
description: The Theater of Aphrodisias dates to the second half of the first century B.C. and stands on the eastern slope of the acropolis.

name: Theater of Assuras
description: The Roman theater of Assuras (Zanfour, Tunisia).

name: Theater of Cyrene
description: The Greek theater of Cyrene was constructed in the sixth century BCE and transformed into a Roman amphitheater in the second century CE.

name: Theater of Delos
description: An ancient theater located in the settlement of Delos.

name: Theater of Dionysus Eleuthereus
description: Theatre on the south slope of the Acropolis in Athens.

name: Theater of Ephesus
description: The Theater of Ephesus sits at the foot of the Panayir mountains. It could host up to 25,000 spectators.

name: Theater of Hippo Regius
description: The Theater of Hippo Regius is perhaps the largest Roman theater in North Africa. It was built in the second century CE.

name: Theater of Melos
description: The Theater of Melos likely has Hellenistic origins but the visible phase belongs to the Roman period.

name: Theater of Miletus
description: The Theater of Miletus was built in four phases between ca. 300 and ca. 133 B.C.

name: theater of Nysa
description: The ancient theater of Nysa could accommodate ca. 12,000 spectators.

name: Theater of Orchomenus
description: A theater of the late fourth century B.C.

name: theater of Peltuinum
description: The Roman theater of Peltuinum.

name: Theater of Pergamon
description: The Greek Theater of Pergamon dates ca. 225 -200 B.C.

name: Theater of Priene
description: Greek theater just below the agora in the Ionian city of Priene.

name: Theater of Pyrrhus
description: The Hellenistic theater at Dodona is linked to the reign of Pyrrhus in the third century B.C.

name: Theater of Sagalassos
description: The Theater of Sagalassos dates to the later second century.

name: Theater of Stratos
description: The Theater of Stratos dates to the late fourth century B.C.

name: Theater of Tegea
description: The remains of the Hellenistic theater of Tegea are to be found beneath the tenth-century A.D. Middle Byzantine church of Palaia Episkopi.

name: Theater of the Muses
description: A third- to second-century B.C. theater connected with the sanctuary of the Muses.

name: Theater of Tigranocerta
description: Theatre, Tigranocerta, Arzen

name: Theater of Tios
description: The ancient theater of Tios.

name: Theater of Zea
description: Visible remains of a Hellenistic theater located to the west of the Zea Harbor in Piraeus, dating ca. 200 B.C.

name: Theater of Zeugma
description: The remains of the ancient theater at Zeugma

name: Theater-temple complex, Monte San Nicola
description: A theater-temple complex that stands within pre-Roman walls belonging to a period of Samnite occupation on Monte San Nicola in the municipality of Pietravairano.

name: Théâtre antique de Tarquimpol
description: The ancient theater of Tarquimpol is located at the ancient settlement of Decempagi, present-day Tarquimpol, in the Moselle department. The theater was revealed by aerial photography in 1981; the theater measures approximately 117 meters in diameter and could accommodate up to 16,000 people.

name: Théâtre Antique de Vaison-la-Romaine
description: The Roman Theater of Vasio Vocontiorum.

name: Théatre Gallo-Romains
description: The so-called Théatre Gallo-Romains could accommodate some 6,600 spectators.

name: Theatre of Apollonia
description: The theatre of the ancient city of Apollonia, situated just outside the city wall, on the east side. The construction appears to be late Hellenistic, with half of the seats cut out of the rock on the elevation of the acropolis, and the other half built up.

name: Theatre of Messene
description: The ancient Theater of Messene (Mavrommati, Messenia, Greece).

name: Theatre of Palmyra
description: Theatre in the Ancient City of Palmyra.

name: Theatre of Parium
description: A Greek theatre at Parium that was reconstructed by the Romans in the second and early third century.

name: Theatrum Pompei
description: Rome's first permanent theater, located in the Campus Martius. The complex was completed under the second consulship of Pompeius Magnus in 55 B.C.

name: Thignica amphitheater
description: A Roman amphitheater at Thignica.

name: Tomen y Mur amphitheatre
description: An oval, embanked area of the north-east of the Roman fort has been identified as an amphitheater, one of only three known in Wales. The amphitheater is located near the site of the parade ground.

name: Tralles ancient theater
description: The theater of ancient Tralles.

name: Uluzibbira Roman amphitheater
description: A Roman amphitheater located in the modern Essed-Nord village, west of Sidi Bou Ali in Tunisia's Sousse governorate.

name: Urso theater
description: The Roman theater of Urso.

name: Verulamium Roman theater
description: The Roman Theatre at Verulamium, St. Albans was begun around the middle of the second century and continues in use until the fourth century.

name: Yalvac Theatre - Antioch Pisidia
description: The theater at Pisidian Antioch was perhaps founded in the first century BCE.

name: Parthian House 139
description: Remains of a house whose earliest components date (C14) to the Parthian era, located south of the large site of Shahr-i Gholghola in the modern Chahar Burjak region of Afghanistan's Nimruz province.

name: House M7-W
description: Private house located in Block M7 of Dura-Europos.

name: House of the Skeleton
description: The so-called "House of the Skeleton" was constructed in the early first century BCE in the Roman colony of Cosa. It stands an example of the domestic architecture of the Late Roman Republic.

name: Building (house?) preceding Synagogue phase 1
description: Traces of preceding building of uncertain type (house?) detected below the synagogue levels in Block L7.

name: Military compound in the Temple of Artemis Azzanathkona
description: A complex of rooms requisitioned for military record keeping and administration, formerly belonging to the Temple of Artemis Azzanathkona.

name: Building (house?) at Dura-Europos preceding Synagogue phase 1
description: Traces of preceding building of uncertain type (house?) detected below the synagogue levels in Block L7.

name: Late Roman Round Building
description: A round building constructed in the northwestern corner of the Athenian Agora in the 5th century AD over the Crossroads Enclosure. It was ruined in the late 5th or 6th century AD.

name: Mirabel Fortress
description: A Crusader fortress, the ruins of which were used to build a 19th-century fortified manor house. They lie over the site of ancient Aphekou Pyrgos in the modern Migdal Tzedek National Park, Israel.

name: Building IV on the Athenian Acropolis
description: A building at the southeast end of the Acropolis of Athens, built perhaps in the 440s BC. Suggested identifications include the workshop for construction of the Parthenon, the hero shrine of Pandion (the eponymous hero of the tribe Pandionis), and the Chalkotheke. It is now covered by the Old Acropolis Museum.

name: Building III on the Athenian Acropolis
description: A building on the north side of the Acropolis of Athens with an attached courtyard, built in the second half of the 5th century BC. It is perhaps to be identified with the house of the Arrhephoroi, but other identifications have been proposed.

name: Rotes Haus (Tell Schech Hamad)
description: The so-called "Red House" is a large building at Dūr-Katlimmu that was built and occupied between the sixth and fifth centuries BCE. The structure included administrative, reception, and living areas, and a few Assyrian cuneiform texts were recovered during its excavation.

name: East Building
description: A building in the southeastern corner of the Athenian Agora, built in the mid-second century BC as the eastern side of the South Square complex and its main entrance from the Panathenaic Way. It was destroyed in the Sullan Sack of 86 BC, but repaired in the Imperial Period.

name: Arcuated Building
description: A mid-first century CE building located east of the Roman Agora in Athens, of unknown purpose. Early 20th-century scholars incorrectly identified it as the Agoranomeion (office of the market inspectors).

name: Market Hall at Aspendos
description: A large, Hellenistic structure at Aspendos subsequently reused as the foundation for a Roman-era basilica that seems to have had a presumably public market and grain storage function.

name: Principia
description: Military headquarters building at Dura-Europos.

name: Building (Temple?) West of The Principia Court (Rooms E7-20, E7-21, E7-22, E7-37, E7-38, E7-39, E7-40)
description: Building located west of the principia in block E7 at Dura-Europos.

name: Late Roman Building
description: A rectangular structure in the Athenian Agora, consisting of 11 rooms surrounding a central courtyard, built in the early 5th century AD in connection with the Palace of the Giants. It appears to have been an administrative building.

name: Sanctuary of Aglauros
description: A shrine dedicated to Aglauros, on the east slope of the Acropolis of Athens. The location was identified by an inscription found in situ.

name: Tullianum
description: Prison located at the foot of the Capitoline Hill, named either for Servius Tullius (578–535 BCE) or for the spring (tullus) on which it was constructed. Also known as the Mamertine Prison. The church of San Giuseppe dei Falegnami was constructed on the site in the 11th century CE.

name: Building V on the Athenian Acropolis
description: A utilitarian building constructed in the southeast corner of the Acropolis of Athens in the first half of the 5th century BC and torn down to make way for Building IV later in the same century.

name: Northwest Building
description: The Northwest Building on the Acropolis of Athens was built in the fifth century BC, probably in the 430s BC, north of the Propylaia. It may have been intended as a banqueting hall, but was never completed and remained an open-air courtyard, perhaps used for storage and staging purposes.

name: Rujm al-Ḥenū
description: Two stone buildings (west and east) located 30 m apart were excavated at modern Rujm al-Ḥenū in Jordan. The pottery found has been dated largely to Iron Age IIc.

props.ancientName: Rujm al-Ḥenū, rajm alhanu, Rujm el Ḥinū; Rujm al Hinu

name: Poros Building
description: A narrow structure southwest of the Agora of Athens, built in the mid-5th century BC. Some modern scholars identify it as the Athenian prison where Sokrates and others were held and executed, but it was probably a workshop for marble workers.

name: House D1
description: A large private house located in Block D1 of Dura-Europos.

name: Six Windows Building Complex at Sikait
description: A complex of three stone-built structures in the ancient settlement of Sikait in Egypt's Eastern Desert. The structure seems to have served a primarily residential function, certainly during the 4th and 5th centuries CE and (in a previous phase) during an as-yet undetermined earlier period. It may also have had a commercial function.

name: Insula dei Dipinti
description: A Hadrianic insula in Ostia Antica between the north wall of the castrum and the Tiber, comprising the houses of Jove and Ganymede, of the Infant Bacchus, and of the Paintings, arranged according to an L-shaped plan. Its façade faced onto two streets, forming a corner, while its inner sides looked out onto a large garden, without porticoes.

name: Masseria Muro
description: Masseria Muro (comune di Mesagne, province of Brindisi, south Italy) is the findspot of a hoard of ancient coins. In 1976, a deposit of coins originating from a number of Greek cities of south Italy was discovered. The coins range in date from the Archaic to the Hellenistic periods.

name: Graecostasis
description: The Graecostasis was located at the margin of the comitium in the Forum Romanum. It was a tribunal where foreign ambassadors would wait.

name: Goeblange
description: The remains of a Roman villa, as well as other structures, have been found in the Miecher forest.

name: Bouleuterion
description: The bouleuterion at Priene, located northeast of the agora, dates ca. 200 BCE.

name: Aiakeion
description: The Aiakeion was a sanctuary of Aiakos built in Athens in the early fifth century BC, which is identified with the Rectangular Peribolos in the southwestern corner of the Agora. In 374/3 BC it was converted to a granary. It was damaged in the Sullan Sack in 86 BC and in the Imperial period it was a pottery workshop.

name: Pompeion
description: A building between the Dipylon Gate and the Sacred Gate used for preparing the Panathenaic procession during the Classical and Hellenistic periods, destroyed during the Sullan Sack in 86 B.C., restored ca. A.D. 144, and destroyed again in the Herulian Sack of A.D. 267.

name: House of the Arrhephoroi
description: A house on the Acropolis of Athens, the residence of the Arrheporoi ('bearers of unspoken things'), two girls, who helped weave Athena's cloak for the Panathenaia festival and carried a box containing the 'unspoken things' down from the Acropolis to a precinct near the sanctuary of Aphrodite in the Gardens during the Arrhephoria festival. The house is possibly to be identified with Building III on the Acropolis.

name: Minoan structure on Vigla Hill
description: Hilltop structure in the Mesara Plain in south-central Crete in use during throughout the Minoan period. Two Tholos tombs are located north of the building.

name: Cold Knap Roman Building
description: A courtyard building of a non-military nature dating to the later Roman period (second to third century CE) was located on the seafront at modern Barry (Wales, United Kingdom).

name: Bagras Castle
description: Bagras Castle is located in Turkey's İskenderun district in the Amanus Mountains. The tenth-century castle was rebuilt in the twelfth century.

name: Anduarsalia
description: A fortress in the vicinity of Til-garimmu fortified by the Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721-705 BC) against Urarṭu.

name: Luhsu
description: A fortress in the vicinity of Til-garimmu fortified by the Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721-705 BC) against Urarṭu.

name: Castellammare della Bruca
description: A fortified medieval settlement that succeeds the acropolis of ancient Velia.

name: Weeting Castle
description: A twelfth-century, moated, fortified manor in Weeting, Norfolk, near Brandon, Suffolk, England.

name: Unnamed Fortification on the Anastasian Wall
description: A fort or tower associated with the Anastasian Wall in Thrace.

name: Unnamed Fortification on the Anastasian Wall
description: A fort or tower associated with the Anastasian Wall in Thrace.

name: Unnamed Fortification on the Anastasian Wall
description: A fort or tower associated with the Anastasian Wall in Thrace.

name: Unnamed Fortification on the Anastasian Wall
description: A fort or tower associated with the Anastasian Wall in Thrace.

name: Büyük Bedesten Wall Fort
description: A fort abutting the Anastasian Wall, 5km west of Çiftlikköy Merkez and 6km northeast of Gümüşpınar in Turkey.

name: Unlabeled feature captured
description: Unlabeled feature captured from the Barrington Atlas

name: Nether Denton Roman fort
description: A Roman fort site at Nether Denton that was identified via cropmarks. The fort formed part of the Stanegate frontier system, and thus pre-dates Hadrian's Wall. It was abandoned ca. 122.

name: Ladle Hill Iron Age Fort
description: Ladle Hill is an Iron Age hill fort located in north Hampshire, England.

name: The Frith
description: The Frith is an Iron Age hillfort in Hampshire, England, that covers 1.6 hectares.

name: Flower's Barrow
description: Flower's Barrow hill fort overlooks Worbarrow Bay in Dorset on England's south coast.

name: Ag. Marina
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 58 G2 Ag. Marina

name: Maiden Castle Roman Fortlet (Cumbria)
description: A Roman fortlet located near Brough in eastern Cumbria (England).

name: Anmurru
description: A fortress in the vicinity of Til-garimmu fortified by the Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721-705 BC) against Urarṭu.

name: Analib(l)a
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 64 H1 Analib(l)a

description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 87 F1 (Sten)Nitike

name: Roman baths at Oescus
description: The Roman baths at Oescus are dated to the middle of the third century.

description: A tower associated with Pompeii's fortification wall that is also sometimes designated as "Tower XI".

name: Unnamed Fort
description: Unlabeled feature captured from the Barrington Atlas

name: Castle of Serravalle
description: The Castle of Serravalle is a twelfth century castle located in Bosa, province of Oristano, Sardinia. It is connected with the Tuscan Malaspina family.

name: Araghju
description: A prehistoric site of the Torrean civilization in Corsica.

name: Novaya Beden'ga I
description: Hillfort site in the watershed between the Volga and Sviyaga rivers. The site shows signs of habitation in the Late Scythian period (1st-3rd centuries CE) and the Hunnish period (4th-6th centuries CE).

name: Torre Scribla
description: The remains of two towers of a Norman fortress near Spezzano Albanese.

name: Fort E of Bida
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 31 A3 unnamed unlabeled (settlements)

name: Fort W of Celtianis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 31 E3 unnamed unlabeled (settlements)

name: Ad Gall(inaci)um?
description: An ancient place located along the road that led from Carthage to Utica, placing it in the vicinity of the modern Sebbalam, Tunisia.

name: Penteskouphia
description: A hill surmounted by a Frankish fortress located ca. 1200 m SW of Akrocorinth.

name: Ninfeo della Lucchina
description: A nymphaeum of the third century CE that was identified in 1990 and was likely one component of a larger villa complex in the north-west suburbium of imperial Rome.

name: Pen Coed-foel Camp
description: Pencoedfoel is the location of an ancient hill fort in Wales.

name: Afyonkarahisar Castle
description: Afyonkarahisar Castle is a Late Bronze Age fort (ca. 1350 B.C.) and may be the site of the Hittite settlement known as Hapanuwa.

name: Barcombe Hill Roman Signal Station
description: A Roman signal station on Barcombe Hill, overlooking the Stanegate and Vindolanda.

name: Castra Bonnensia
description: The Roman legionary base at Bonna.

name: Gors Fawr stone circle
description: Gors Fawr Stone Circle is located in Mynachlog-Ddu, Pembrokeshire, Wales, United Kingdom.

name: Habıbuşağı (İzoli)
description: Habıbuşağı (İzoli) was an Urartian fortress situated near Kömürhan, at the left shore of the river Euphrates in the Elazığ Province of eastern Turkey. Today it is covered by the reservoir behind the Karakaya dam. Habıbuşağı is best known on account of a rock inscription of the Urartian king Sarduri II, son of Argišti (eighth century BCE) which reports the expedition of Sarduri to this region. Habıbuşağı is, in fact, the westernmost known fortress of the Urartian kingdom.

name: (B)Ossia/Ad Dianam?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 G2 (B)Ossia/Ad Dianam?

name: Isthmia Fortress
description: A Byzantine fortress erected in the fifth century CE that includes three gates and 21 towers.

name: Castle of Bominaco
description: A castle at Bominaco in Italy that originally dates to the twelfth century.

name: Amasya castle
description: Amasya Castle or Harşene Castle, is a fortress located in Amasya in northern Turkey.

name: Cozzo Presepe
description: Cozzo Presepe is an archaeological site located in the Basilicata region of Italy, specifically in the comune of Montescaglioso. Several archaeological features have been identified at the site, including an unusual defensive wall dating ca. 300 BCE.

name: Maen Castle
description: Maen Castle is an Iron Age promontory fort near Land's End in Cornwall.

name: Dorikon
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 C3 Dorikon

name: Ghuzza Roman Fort
description: A roman-era fort at Al-Ghuzza in Egypt's eastern desert.

name: Castell Henllys
description: Castell Henllys is an Iron Age hill fort located in north Pembrokeshire, Wales.

description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 87 A4 Kizari

description: A turret situated on Hadrian's Wall.

name: Munychia
description: A hill in the Piraeus and the site of extensive fortifications in the Classical and Hellenistic periods.

name: Byzantine fortress, Ammaedara
description: The Byzantine fortress at Ammaedara is a border fortress located in the far west of the Byzacena. It was built between 534 and 565 and covers 2.55 ha.

name: Tor de' Schiavi
description: The Tor de' Schiavi is a medieval tower (thirteenth century) built atop the remains of the octagonal hall of the Villa Gordiani.

name: Pizzo S. Angelo
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 E3 Pizzo S. Angelo

name: Ksar Douiret
description: Fortified Berber granary (ksar). The surviving structure dates to the 14th century CE, but the site may have been in use as early as the Roman era.

name: Kundu/Kyinda
description: Kundu is mentioned in Akkadian royal inscriptions of the seventh-century-BC Assyrian king Esarhaddon. In Classical times, Strabo describes Kyinda as a fortress where Macedonian kings deposited treasure. Eumenes reportedly captured the treasure when he revolted against Antigonus. The site is now unlocated.

name: Beacon Hill Hillfort
description: Beacon Hill Hill fort is an Iron Age hill fort in Hampshire, England.

name: Kainys (fort)
description: A Hellenistic fort located on the Kainys promontory (modern Punta del Pezzo) in southern Italy.

name: S. Eufemia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 46 C5 S. Eufemia

name: Castello di Palma di Montechiaro
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 D4 Castello di Palma di Montechiaro

name: Torre dei Capocci
description: The Torre dei Capocci, together with the Torre dei Graziani, was built in the twelfth century by the Arcioni family as a monumentalized entrance to the Esquiline.

name: Badia'
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 78 C3 Badia'

name: Deir Mustafa Kashef
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 79 D3 Deir Mustafa Kashef

name: Kalalat 1
description: The site of a Roman fort in the eastern desert of Egypt.

name: Nakheil
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 80 E1 Nakheil (1)

name: Pumsaint Roman Fort (ancient Louentinon?)
description: A Roman fort located in and around the modern village of Pumsaint in Wales, it is probably the "Louentinon" mentioned by Ptolemy. The Latinate form "Luentinum" is common in the literature but is not attested by any ancient source.

name: Boon
description: An ancient Egyptian settlement located on the West bank of the Nile to the North of the Second Cataract. During the Old Kingdom Boon or Buhen was the site of a trading colony. During the Twelfth Dynasty, a large fortress was built during the reign of Senusret III.

name: Iken
description: An ancient settlement located in Sudan's Northern state. The ancient site was situated at the 2nd cataract in Wadi Halfa and contained one of the largest fortresses in Nubia. The site was inundated by the Aswan High dam hydroelectric project.

name: ‘Nemetotacio’
description: The earthwork remains of a Roman fort that likely covered an area of approximately 2 ha.

name: Portus ‘Adurni’?
description: Portus ‘Adurni’? was likely a third-century A.D. Roman fort (now Portchester) mentioned by name only in the Notitia Dignitatum. The Roman fort was converted to a Saxon castle and is thus well preserved.

name: Tamion?
description: Tamion? (modern Cardiff, Wales). A series of Roman forts occupied the same site in Cardiff, located on a river terrace adjacent to the River Taff, and represent continuous occupation from the late 50s AD until the late fourth century.

name: Fort at Akrite Ins.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 D3 unnamed fort (on Akrite)

name: Arsham

name: Palaikastro

name: Plakoto fort
description: Described by Ober as a "signal tower and small fortification", Plakoto fort is situated at the northern edge of the Thriasian plain.

name: Rusahinili

name: Castra of Drobeta

name: Zereia/Zeira
description: Diodorus refers to Zereia/Zeira as a fortress and places it near the location of Chalcidice. It was razed by Philip V. It is now unlocated. (cited: BAtlas 51 unlocated Zereia/Zeira)

name: Wilderness Plantation Roman fortlet
description: A fortlet was identified via aerial photographs in October 1965. In this vicinity, the Antonine Wall runs roughly parallel between the River Kelvin to the north and the Forth and Clyde Canal to the south.

name: Castrum Caetani
description: A Medieval fortification that incorporates and repurposes the Roman mausoleum of Caecilia Metella.

name: Burgus Szentendre-Dera
description: Burgus Szentendre-Dera is a late antique Roman military encampment located along the Hungarian Danube at Szentendre (St. Andrae). The fort secured the Pannonian Limes.

name: Baths of the Old Convent, Tharros
description: Roman baths at Tharros known as the "Baths of the Old Convent".

description: A late Roman burgus (10,40 × 10,20 m) at Visegrád-Szentgyörgypuszta

name: Ušnanis
description: Ušnanis is a Quean fortress located on the Cilician plain. In the eighth century BC, perhaps only for a short time, Phrygia controlled the territory in which Ušnanis was located. The Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721-705), after his armies defeated the Phrygian king Midas (= Mitâ of Musku), restored Ušnanis to Que, which was then a province of the Assyrian Empire.

name: Arab Baths of Girona
description: A bath complex at Girona built in the Romanesque style for King Alfons I in 1194.

name: Grand Lagneux Roman Bath
description: At Grand Lagneux, a small village in the French commune of Yenne, a rescue excavation in 2020 revealed part of a Roman-era bathing establishment. The excavated site is now buried under a modern home. This find constitutes another possible locality in the commune for attested ancient name "Etanna".

name: Can Blai
description: A Late Roman fortlet on the island of Formentera.

name: Roman baths, Spoleto
description: Roman baths located at Spoletium.

name: Castello Piccolomini
description: A castle of the fourteenth and fifteenth centuries located in Celano, the Province of L'Aquila (Abruzzo), Italy.

name: Torre Maggiore
description: A place located at the 21st km of the via Ardeatina, Torre Maggiore is likely built on the site of a domusculta and is connected to key Roman families, including the Savelli, Altieri, Capodiferro, Serlupi, and Maddaleni families. The tower has a square plan and rises to 34 meters.

name: Mooghaun Ringfort
description: A first millennium B.C. ringfort in County Clare, Ireland, with three concentric rings of fortifications. At 27 acres, this is one of the largest hill forts in Iron Age Europe.

name: Costești-Cetățuie
description: Costești-Cetățuie Dacian fortress.

name: Torre de los Moros
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 F4 Torre de los Moros

name: Torre dei Conti
description: A fortified Medieval tower located in Rome, dating to AD 1238, and built by Richard Conti, brother of Pope Innocent III.

name: Trenčin castle
description: A castle located above the town of Trenčín in western Slovakia that preserves epigraphic evidence of Roman military activity during the Marcomannic Wars.

name: Khirbet Qeiyafa
description: Khirbet Qeiyafa, also known as Elah Fortress, is an Iron Age fortress site located about 40 kilometers southwest of Jerusalem, overlooking the Elah Valley. The site was likely constructed in the late eleventh century BCE and destroyed or abandoned in the mid- to late tenth century BCE.

name: Byzantine fortress at Timgad
description: A sixth century A.D. Byzantine fortress built at Timgad by the governor Solomon.

name: Purṭir
description: A fortress in the vicinity of Til-garimmu fortified by the Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721-705 BC) against Urarṭu.

name: Roman siege camp
description: A siege camp dating to the Roman siege of the fortress of Masada in A.D. 72.

name: Cahercommaun
description: A triple stone ringfort built ca. AD 800.

name: Qal’at Salah El-Din/Fortress of Saladin
description: An ancient castle, located in modern Syria. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2006 as part of the "Crac des Chevaliers and Qal’at Salah El-Din" listing.

name: Rocca San Silvestro, Campiglia Marittima
description: The Rocca San Silvestro is a tenth century fortified village on Monte Rombolo near Campiglia Marittima in the Alta Maremma. The village was built in order to exploit rich mineral resources in the area.

name: Zahhak Castle
description: A fortified citadel located in Hashtrud, East Azerbaijan Province, Iran. It takes its name from the mythological figure Zahhak. The site is in use from the second millennium BC until the Timurid era.

name: Nurabad
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 B3 Nurabad

name: Maesbury Castle
description: Maesbury Castle is a small ring hillfort in the Mendip Hills in Somerset, England.

name: Chûn Castle

name: Kef Kalesi
description: Kef Kalesi is an Urartian fortress near Adilcevaz in the area of Lake Van in the southeast of Turkey. According to an Urartian inscription of Rusa, son of Argišti II (7th century BCE), it was built by this very king. The inscription further mentions the foundation of a new city at this site which Rusa named "City of the God Haldi in the Land Ziuquni".

name: Castello di Sant'Alessio
description: A tenth-century castle located near the di Sant'Alessio in Sicily.

name: Alanya Castle

name: Rederzhausen

description: A Medieval tower along the Via Flaminia near Prima Porta.

name: Old Oswestry

name: Torre Valca
description: A Medieval tower (12th or 13th century) is now located within the grounds of the Caffarella park.

name: Myrtonion
description: A fortress in Thrace, whose precise location is now unknown.

name: Hedemünden
description: An early Imperial fort located on the river Werra.

name: Oram's Arbour
description: Oram's Arbour, now a park, was the site of Iron Age occupation prior to the establishment of Venta. Location based on OpenStreetMap.

name: Hellenistic Baths (Syracuse)
description: A Hellenistic bath complex at Syracuse, located near the Greek theater.

name: Roman baths (Capsa)
description: Epigraphically and archaeologically attested bath complex at Capsa (modern Gafsa) in Tunisia. Two interconnected, open-air quadrangular pools, known collectively today as the "Roman basins" (Piscine romaine) remain. They were historically supplied by a mineral spring known today as Ain Dar el Bey.

name: Sela
description: Sela (also as-Silaʿ and es-Selaʿ) was the the capital of the land Edom. The city is equated with the biblical Joktheel/Jokteel/Jectehel.

name: La Loggia
description: "La Loggia" refers to a squared tower that is a part of the polygonal masonry curtain wall of Norba.

name: Unnamed fortification
description: An ancient fortification located northwest of Avlan Gölü. This place is discussed by Bean in regard to identifying the site of ancient Podalia in Lycia.

name: Crac des Chevaliers
description: An ancient castle, located in modern Syria. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2006 as part of the "Crac des Chevaliers and Qal’at Salah El-Din" listing.

name: Tour Magne
description: Situated atop Mount Cavalier, the so-called "Tour Magne" is a tower on the fortified walls of ancient Nemausus. The tower dates to the time of Augustus.

name: Torre di Donoratico

name: Whitemoss Roman auxiliary fort

name: Tino di Moscona

name: Byrsa
description: Walled citadel in Carthage built in the early first millennium BCE. The site was destroyed during the Third Punic War in 146 BCE by the forces of Scipio Aemilianus. The hill became the site of the city's forum in the Roman period.

name: Büyükkale

name: Bindon Hill
description: Bindon Hill is the site of extensive Iron Age earthworks on a coastal hill area in Dorset, England.

name: Broch of Mousa
description: The Broch of Mousa is a drystone broch in Scotland, dating ca. 400-200 BC.

name: Worlebury Camp
description: Worlebury Camp is the site of an Iron Age hill fort in Somerset, England.

name: So-called Caesareum of Cyrene
description: A large architectural complex on the east side of the acropolis ridge in Cyrene that was probably built at the beginning of the first century AD and used as a gymnasium, but was later repurposed as forum in Flavian times, when the rooms north of the complex were levelled in order to build a large basilica. Various ruins can still be seen, including the podium of a temple in the central area, possibly dedicated to Dionysos. A fragmentary inscription referring to the "portico of Caesar", found over the inner side of the porch, made the place known as Caesareum.

name: Agia Triada Tower
description: A fortified architectural complex on the island of Amorgos that dates to the fourth century BCE.

name: Theater of Aigeira
description: The ancient Greek Theater of Aigeira overlooks the Gulf of Corinth. The theater was built ca. 280-250 B.C.E. and enjoys a remarkable state of preservation.

name: So-called Temple of Saturn (Sezze)
description: The ruins of an architectural complex in Sezze that acquired the misnomer of "Temple of Saturn" in the early modern period. The ruins are likely those of a platform villa and include visible remains in opus incertum and polygonal masonry of Lugli's "fourth" typological category, meaning the blocks approach isodomic forms.

name: Greek theater, Monte Iato
description: The Greek theater of Monte Iato was built in the fourth century BCE at a site already inhabited by the Elymians. The theater complex had a long life, used for the same purpose in the Roman period and repurposed as a domestic space by Arab occupiers in the seventh century.

name: Roman theater
description: The theater at Thugga dates to 168 or 169 and could accommodate 3500 spectators.

name: So-called Theatre 2
description: The ruins of an ancient theatre in Cyrene, known as "Theatre 2".

name: Roman theater of Alba Fucens
description: The Roman theater of Alba Fucens

name: Roman Odeon of Gortyn
description: The Roman Odeon of Gortyn dates to the first century.

name: Bouleuterion (Troy)
description: The ruins identified as a bouleuterion at Ilium lie about 70 meters from the odeion. The foundations of this complex overlay, in part, the walls of Troy VI.

name: Old Palace at Assur
description: The Old Palace was the most important palace complex in the city of Assur. Although the oldest, securely-datable archaeological existence for the palace dates to the early Middle Assyrian period, this royal residence was probably founded in the Old Assyrian period, possibly during the reign of Šamšī-Adad I (ca. 1836–1776 BC). This important building was continuously until the sack and destruction of Assur in 614 BC. The Old Palace not only served as a residence for the Assyrian king and his family and an administrative center, but it was also a place of worship where the gods gathered on special occasions and the burial place of Assyrian kings.

name: Roman theater of Fioccaglia
description: Crop marks, confirmed by a magnetometer survey reported in 2026, demonstrate the location and footprint of a monumental theater at the archaeological site of Fioccaglia di Fumeri.

name: Aginnum
description: Aginnum (modern Agen) was the capital of the Nitiobriges in Gaul.

name: Il Barco
description: The so-called "Barco Borghese" is an archaeological site located in Monte Porzio Catone (Roma) in Italy. This multiperiod site began as a Roman terracing or substructure, perhaps originally meant to support a villa (basis villae). Subsequent modification and reuse converted the Roman structure into a "barco", an enclosure for animal hunts.
